questions: Polyuria, polydipsia, weight change, blurred vision, infections.
ASCVD, CKD, HF, neuropathy, retinopathy, sleep apnea, steroid exposure.
What is the current trajectory, baseline status, prior diagnosis date, and what changed today?
Which medications, supplements, missed doses, recent procedures, acute illnesses, pregnancy/fertility context, or assay-interfering factors could change interpretation?
Ask about hypoglycemia, hyperglycemia symptoms, sick-day triggers, insulin/medication access, steroid or SGLT2 inhibitor exposure, and recent infection.
Clarify ASCVD, CKD, neuropathy, retinopathy, foot ulcer/wound, pregnancy, meal pattern, and technology use because these change targets and therapy.
exam: BP, BMI/waist, volume if symptomatic.
Foot skin/pulses/monofilament or vibration when neuropathy risk.
Cardiovascular and thyroid exam when indicated.
Vitals and acuity screen: BP, HR, respiratory status, temperature when acute, weight/BMI or waist when cardiometabolic risk matters.
Targeted complication screen guided by symptoms: volume/perfusion, mental status, skin/mucosa, neuromuscular findings, and organ-system complications.
Hydration, mucous membranes, orthostasis/perfusion, respiratory pattern, and mental status when acute hyperglycemia, DKA/HHS, or hypoglycemia is possible.
Feet/skin when diabetes complications, neuropathy, infection, ulcer, vascular disease, or discharge safety is relevant.
tests: A1c, fasting plasma glucose, or 2-hour OGTT to confirm.
CMP/eGFR, urine albumin-creatinine ratio, fasting lipids.
Ketones/anion gap if vomiting, weight loss, dehydration, or acute symptoms.
Compare with prior results and repeat discordant nonurgent endocrine tests using correct timing, local assay, and interference precautions.

Diabetes safety tests: review POC/plasma glucose trend, BMP with potassium/bicarbonate/anion gap and creatinine, beta-hydroxybutyrate or urine ketones when acutely ill, and UACR/lipids for complication-directed therapy.
A1c or plasma glucose criteria for diagnosis/classification, plus CGM/glucometer pattern when available.
CMP/eGFR, urine albumin-creatinine ratio, lipids, and ketones/anion gap/beta-hydroxybutyrate when acute symptoms or insulin deficiency are possible.
reference_values: A1c: normal <5.7%; prediabetes 5.7-6.4%; diabetes >=6.5%.
Fasting plasma glucose: normal <100 mg/dL; prediabetes 100-125; diabetes >=126.
2-hour 75-g OGTT: normal <140 mg/dL; prediabetes 140-199; diabetes >=200.
Random plasma glucose >=200 mg/dL with classic symptoms supports diabetes diagnosis.
UACR normal <30 mg/g; moderately increased 30-300; severely increased >300.
Use local laboratory intervals and assay-specific cutoffs; endocrine immunoassays are affected by pregnancy, age, sex, acute illness, binding proteins, biotin, and medication effects.
red_flags: DKA/HHS symptoms, dehydration, vomiting, altered mental status.
Random glucose >=200 mg/dL with classic symptoms.
Hemodynamic instability, altered mental status, severe electrolyte/glucose abnormality, arrhythmia, airway compromise, or vision threat requires urgent escalation.
DKA/HHS physiology, recurrent severe hypoglycemia, vomiting/dehydration, altered mental status, infected foot wound, or pregnancy with hyperglycemia.
management_changes: Diabetes-range A1c/FPG/OGTT changes diagnosis and treatment intensity.
UACR >=30 or reduced eGFR changes kidney-protective therapy.
Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care.
Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment.
Ketosis/acidosis, HHS osmolality, or severe hypoglycemia triggers urgent protocolized treatment rather than routine outpatient adjustment.
CKD, albuminuria, ASCVD/HF risk, pregnancy, foot infection, or insulin access barriers change medication choice and follow-up urgency.
decision_steps: Diagnose with A1c >=6.5%, fasting plasma glucose >=126 mg/dL, 2-hour OGTT >=200 mg/dL, or random glucose >=200 mg/dL with classic symptoms; repeat confirmatory testing if asymptomatic.
At diagnosis, classify ASCVD, heart failure, CKD, obesity, hypoglycemia risk, medication access, and pregnancy possibility before selecting therapy.
Screen complications with eGFR, urine albumin-creatinine ratio, lipids, BP, foot neuropathy/perfusion exam, and eye/retinopathy follow-up.
If vomiting, dehydration, weight loss, ketones, acidosis, or very high glucose is present, route to DKA/HHS evaluation instead of routine outpatient titration.
Confirm that Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus is the selected clinical intent, then screen for red flags before routine outpatient testing.

Order the first-line tests in sequence: A1c, fasting plasma glucose, or 2-hour OGTT to confirm; CMP/eGFR, urine albumin-creatinine ratio, fasting lipids; Ketones/anion gap if vomiting, weight loss, dehydration, or acute symptoms.

Interpret numeric results against the workup cutoffs and local assay ranges: A1c: normal <5.7%; prediabetes 5.7-6.4%; diabetes >=6.5%. Fasting plasma glucose: normal <100 mg/dL; prediabetes 100-125; diabetes >=126.

If the initial result is equivocal, discordant with the phenotype, or affected by medication, acute illness, pregnancy, age, sex, or assay interference, repeat or confirm using the next guideline test before committing to long-term therapy.
treatment_options: Begin individualized lifestyle, diabetes education, weight-management, BP/lipid, tobacco, vaccine, kidney, and cardiovascular risk-reduction plan.
Use metformin when appropriate and add GLP-1 RA or SGLT2 inhibitor preferentially when ASCVD, CKD, heart failure, obesity, or cardiorenal risk dominates.
Use insulin when catabolic symptoms, ketosis, severe symptomatic hyperglycemia, or A1c/glucose level suggests insulin deficiency or urgent control is needed.
Titrate therapy to individualized A1c and glucose goals while monitoring hypoglycemia, kidney function, adverse effects, and access.
Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus; draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization.
For confirmed Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway.
Monitor treatment response with the condition-specific clinical endpoint plus the most relevant labs or imaging listed in this workup.
Escalate to endocrinology or the relevant specialty when results are severe, discordant, recurrent, treatment-limiting, or require localization, surgery, genetic testing, or high-risk medication.

basic_bedside_data_safety_checks: Measure blood pressure: Check and document as baseline bedside data for this workup. Hypotension or severe hypertension changes acuity, medication safety, fluid strategy, and escalation. Rationale: Targets hemodynamic safety, hypertension or hypotension severity, and medication or fluid-safety context; positive, negative, or unable-to-assess findings guide escalation, diagnostic testing, treatment safety, or specialty planning for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Measure heart rate: Check and document as baseline bedside data for this workup. Tachycardia, bradycardia, or irregular rhythm changes acuity, ECG need, and treatment urgency. Rationale: Screens severity and immediate safety findings that can change triage, monitoring, fluids, or urgent escalation for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Measure current weight: Check and document as baseline bedside data for this workup. Weight change influences insulin resistance framing, nutrition plan, and medication selection. Rationale: Quantifies anthropometric and cardiometabolic risk so abnormal values can change diagnostic criteria, treatment selection, dosing context, or prevention intensity for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.
clinical_questions: How much are you drinking and urinating, including polyuria, polydipsia, overnight urination, thirst intensity, access to water, and any dehydration or confusion? Options: No GI/volume symptom / Vomiting / Nausea / Abdominal pain / Poor intake / Cannot keep fluids/meds down / Other ___. Purpose: Defines symptom severity, tempo, complications, and whether Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus needs urgent evaluation today. Management: A positive answer changes urgency from routine diabetes assessment to same-day hyperglycemia, ketosis, dehydration, infection, or insulin-delivery evaluation for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

How has weight changed from baseline, and was the change intentional or associated with appetite, fluid status, or systemic symptoms? Options: Today or acute onset / Days / Weeks to months / Chronic or baseline / Worse today / Unknown / Other ___. Purpose: Defines symptom severity, tempo, complications, and whether Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus needs urgent evaluation today. Management: The answer changes cardiometabolic risk stratification, prevention intensity, complication screening, and follow-up interval for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Any blurred vision, known retinopathy, eye injections/laser, or overdue dilated eye examination? Options: No matching feature / Blurred vision / Known retinopathy / Eye injections/laser / Overdue dilated eye examination / Other ___. Purpose: Defines symptom severity, tempo, complications, and whether Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus needs urgent evaluation today. Management: The answer changes diabetes eye-complication triage, dilated-eye exam referral timing, medication safety, and follow-up intensity for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Any measured fever, rigors, hypothermia, or toxic appearance suggesting infection as a precipitant or mimic? Options: No measured fever / Less than 39 C / 39 C or higher / Chills or rigors / Hypothermia/toxic appearance / Unknown / Other ___. Purpose: Defines the patient-specific context needed to interpret endocrine tests, avoid common mimics, and choose the safest next step for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus. Management: A positive answer raises acuity and can change same-day labs, imaging, monitored setting, or urgent endocrine escalation for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Any cough, sputum, pleuritic pain, dyspnea, hypoxemia, or focal chest symptoms suggesting pneumonia or respiratory infection? Options: No respiratory symptoms / Cough / Sputum / Shortness of breath / Pleuritic pain / Hypoxemia or oxygen need / Other ___. Purpose: Defines symptom severity, tempo, complications, and whether Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus needs urgent evaluation today. Management: A positive answer raises acuity and can change same-day labs, imaging, monitored setting, or urgent endocrine escalation for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Any dysuria, urinary frequency or urgency, suprapubic pain, hematuria, or flank pain suggesting UTI or pyelonephritis? Options: No urinary symptoms / Dysuria / Frequency or urgency / Suprapubic pain / Hematuria / Flank pain / Reduced urine / Other ___. Purpose: Defines symptom severity, tempo, complications, and whether Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus needs urgent evaluation today. Management: The answer changes the leading Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus pathway by refining severity, mimics, confirmatory testing, treatment safety, and follow-up timing. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Any foot ulcer, wound, skin redness, drainage, tenderness, indwelling line/device, or procedure-site concern suggesting infection? Options: No skin or line concern / Redness / Drainage / Wound or ulcer / Line/device concern / Procedure-site concern / Other ___. Purpose: Identifies exposures, physiologic states, or treatment changes that can explain abnormal results or make Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus therapy unsafe. Management: Complication history changes risk staging, prevention priorities, medication selection, and follow-up intensity for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Any history of ASCVD, heart failure, stroke/TIA, peripheral arterial disease, claudication, or vascular procedures? Options: No matching feature / History of ASCVD / Heart failure / Stroke/TIA / Peripheral arterial disease / Claudication / Vascular procedures / Other ___. Purpose: Identifies exposures, physiologic states, or treatment changes that can explain abnormal results or make Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus therapy unsafe. Management: The answer changes cardiometabolic risk stratification, prevention intensity, complication screening, and follow-up interval for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Any numbness, tingling, burning pain, loss of protective sensation, falls, ulcers, or neuropathy diagnosis? Options: No bone/mineral symptom / Low-trauma fracture / Bone or back pain / Height loss / Falls/gait instability / Kidney stones / Malabsorption risk / Other ___. Purpose: Defines symptom severity, tempo, complications, and whether Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus needs urgent evaluation today. Management: The answer changes fracture or mineral-risk stratification, imaging threshold, calcium/vitamin D interpretation, and urgency of treatment for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Any loud snoring, witnessed apneas, morning headaches, daytime sleepiness, resistant hypertension, or CPAP use? Options: No matching feature / Loud snoring / Witnessed apneas / Morning headaches / Daytime sleepiness / Resistant hypertension / CPAP / Other ___. Purpose: Defines symptom severity, tempo, complications, and whether Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus needs urgent evaluation today. Management: The answer changes cardiometabolic risk stratification, prevention intensity, complication screening, and follow-up interval for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.
conditional_history_add_ons: Any oral, injected, inhaled, topical, eye-drop, joint-injection, or supplement steroid exposure? Options: No relevant exposure / Medication change / Supplement use / Missed doses / Steroid or hormone exposure / Biotin / Iodine/contrast exposure / Other ___. Purpose: Identifies exposures, physiologic states, or treatment changes that can explain abnormal results or make Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus therapy unsafe. Management: The answer changes Cushing phenotype probability, exogenous steroid exclusion, screening-test selection, and urgency of complication management for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Do you use a CGM, insulin pump, smart pen, glucometer, or diabetes app, and are the data reliable enough to guide treatment changes? Options: Diabetes type known / Basal/bolus regimen / Pump or CGM use / Last insulin dose known / Missed or reduced insulin / Unknown / Other ___. Purpose: Defines the patient-specific context needed to interpret endocrine tests, avoid common mimics, and choose the safest next step for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus. Management: The answer changes whether glucose data can safely guide medication adjustment, education, device troubleshooting, or escalation for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.
core_physical_exam_maneuvers: Inspect feet for ulcers: Inspect both feet including interdigital spaces and pressure points for ulcer, callus, erythema, drainage, deformity, or infection. Findings: No ulcer / Ulcer/wound / Callus/deformity / Infection concern / Unable to assess. Target: diabetic neuropathy, foot ulcer risk, infection, or peripheral arterial disease Management: An ulcer or wound changes infection workup, offloading, antibiotic planning, and disposition. Source: ADA_SOC_2026; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Palpate pedal pulses: Palpate bilateral pulses at the named sites, comparing amplitude, symmetry, rhythm, and perfusion. Findings: Symmetric/normal / Diminished / Absent / Asymmetric / Unable to assess. Target: diabetic foot perfusion, peripheral arterial disease, or vascular risk Management: Diminished pulses change vascular risk assessment and foot-wound management. Source: ADA_SOC_2026; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Test monofilament sensation: Apply a 10-g monofilament perpendicular to standard plantar sites until it bends, with eyes closed, documenting sites felt or missed. Findings: Protective sensation intact / Reduced / Absent / Unable to assess. Target: diabetic neuropathy, foot ulcer risk, infection, or peripheral arterial disease Management: Loss of protective sensation changes foot-care counseling, footwear planning, and ulcer prevention. Source: ADA_SOC_2026; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment 10-g monofilament
conditional_exam_add_ons: Observe Kussmaul breathing: Observe respiratory rate, depth, accessory muscle use, speech tolerance, and breathing pattern at rest. Findings: Absent / Deep/labored pattern / Respiratory distress / Unable to assess. Target: respiratory compensation, pulmonary congestion, infection, obstruction, or distress Management: Kussmaul breathing changes crisis severity and urgent treatment pathway. Source: ADA_SOC_2026; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Palpate abdomen for tenderness: Inspect and palpate the abdomen gently, localizing tenderness and stopping if guarding, rebound, or severe pain occurs. Findings: Nontender / Tender / Guarding / Rebound/peritoneal concern / Unable to assess. Target: abdominal complication, endocrine crisis mimic, or precipitating illness Management: Tenderness changes precipitant evaluation and concern for surgical or pancreatitis mimic. Source: ADA_SOC_2026; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect insulin pump site: Inspect the infusion or device site for dislodgement, leakage, erythema, tenderness, occlusion, or supply failure. Findings: Site intact / Dislodged/occluded/leaking / Erythema/tenderness/drainage / Unable to assess. Target: insulin delivery failure, infusion-site infection, or device-related precipitant Management: Site failure changes precipitant assessment and insulin delivery plan. Source: ADA_SOC_2026; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect foot wound: Inspect both feet including interdigital spaces and pressure points for ulcer, callus, erythema, drainage, deformity, or infection. Findings: No ulcer / Ulcer/wound / Callus/deformity / Infection concern / Unable to assess. Target: diabetic neuropathy, foot ulcer risk, infection, or peripheral arterial disease Management: A wound changes infection workup, antibiotics, and disposition. Source: ADA_SOC_2026; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect mucous membranes for dehydration: Inspect oral mucosa and tongue moisture with attention to dry mucosa, cracked lips, and overall hydration context. Findings: Moist / Dry / Very dry / Unable to assess. Target: volume status, dehydration severity, or peripheral perfusion Management: Dehydration changes fluid strategy, lab urgency, and monitoring needs. Source: ADA_SOC_2026; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Rationale: Routes Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

If the initial result is equivocal, discordant with the phenotype, or affected by medication, acute illness, pregnancy, age, sex, or assay interference, repeat or confirm using the next guideline test before committing to long-term therapy. red_flags: DKA/HHS symptoms, dehydration, vomiting, altered mental status.: Escalate urgently, reassess severity, and evaluate for dangerous mimics or complications. Rationale: Identifies dangerous Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus presentations that should override routine outpatient workup and prompt urgent escalation. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Random glucose >=200 mg/dL with classic symptoms.: Escalate urgently, reassess severity, and evaluate for dangerous mimics or complications. Rationale: Identifies dangerous Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus presentations that should override routine outpatient workup and prompt urgent escalation. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Hemodynamic instability, altered mental status, severe electrolyte/glucose abnormality, arrhythmia, airway compromise, or vision threat requires urgent escalation.: Escalate urgently, reassess severity, and evaluate for dangerous mimics or complications. Rationale: Identifies dangerous Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus presentations that should override routine outpatient workup and prompt urgent escalation. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

DKA/HHS physiology, recurrent severe hypoglycemia, vomiting/dehydration, altered mental status, infected foot wound, or pregnancy with hyperglycemia.: Escalate urgently, reassess severity, and evaluate for dangerous mimics or complications. Rationale: Identifies dangerous Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus presentations that should override routine outpatient workup and prompt urgent escalation. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.
results_that_change_management: Diabetes-range A1c/FPG/OGTT changes diagnosis and treatment intensity. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.
UACR >=30 or reduced eGFR changes kidney-protective therapy. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_DIAGNOSIS_2026.

Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_DIAGNOSIS_2026.

Ketosis/acidosis, HHS osmolality, or severe hypoglycemia triggers urgent protocolized treatment rather than routine outpatient adjustment. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

CKD, albuminuria, ASCVD/HF risk, pregnancy, foot infection, or insulin access barriers change medication choice and follow-up urgency. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_DIAGNOSIS_2026.
treatment_options: Begin individualized lifestyle, diabetes education, weight-management, BP/lipid, tobacco, vaccine, kidney, and cardiovascular risk-reduction plan. Rationale: Links confirmed Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Use metformin when appropriate and add GLP-1 RA or SGLT2 inhibitor preferentially when ASCVD, CKD, heart failure, obesity, or cardiorenal risk dominates. Rationale: Links confirmed Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ADA_DIAGNOSIS_2026.

Use insulin when catabolic symptoms, ketosis, severe symptomatic hyperglycemia, or A1c/glucose level suggests insulin deficiency or urgent control is needed. Rationale: Links confirmed Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Titrate therapy to individualized A1c and glucose goals while monitoring hypoglycemia, kidney function, adverse effects, and access. Rationale: Links confirmed Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ADA_DIAGNOSIS_2026.

Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus; draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization. Rationale: Links confirmed Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

For confirmed Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway. Rationale: Links confirmed Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ADA_DIAGNOSIS_2026.

questions: Rapid polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss.
Vomiting, abdominal pain, Kussmaul breathing, confusion.
Personal/family autoimmune disease.
What is the current trajectory, baseline status, prior diagnosis date, and what changed today?
Which medications, supplements, missed doses, recent procedures, acute illnesses, pregnancy/fertility context, or assay-interfering factors could change interpretation?
Ask about hypoglycemia, hyperglycemia symptoms, sick-day triggers, insulin/medication access, steroid or SGLT2 inhibitor exposure, and recent infection.
Clarify ASCVD, CKD, neuropathy, retinopathy, foot ulcer/wound, pregnancy, meal pattern, and technology use because these change targets and therapy.
exam: Vitals, hydration, mental status.
Respiratory pattern/Kussmaul concern.
Abdomen and infection/skin trigger exam.
Vitals and acuity screen: BP, HR, respiratory status, temperature when acute, weight/BMI or waist when cardiometabolic risk matters.
Targeted complication screen guided by symptoms: volume/perfusion, mental status, skin/mucosa, neuromuscular findings, and organ-system complications.
Hydration, mucous membranes, orthostasis/perfusion, respiratory pattern, and mental status when acute hyperglycemia, DKA/HHS, or hypoglycemia is possible.
Feet/skin when diabetes complications, neuropathy, infection, ulcer, vascular disease, or discharge safety is relevant.
tests: A1c/glucose criteria plus ketones/acid-base if symptomatic.
Beta-hydroxybutyrate, BMP, venous/arterial pH, bicarbonate, anion gap.
Islet autoantibodies: GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, insulin Ab if needed; C-peptide with concurrent glucose.
Compare with prior results and repeat discordant nonurgent endocrine tests using correct timing, local assay, and interference precautions.

Diabetes safety tests: review POC/plasma glucose trend, BMP with potassium/bicarbonate/anion gap and creatinine, beta-hydroxybutyrate or urine ketones when acutely ill, and UACR/lipids for complication-directed therapy.
A1c or plasma glucose criteria for diagnosis/classification, plus CGM/glucometer pattern when available.
CMP/eGFR, urine albumin-creatinine ratio, lipids, and ketones/anion gap/beta-hydroxybutyrate when acute symptoms or insulin deficiency are possible.
reference_values: A1c: normal <5.7%; prediabetes 5.7-6.4%; diabetes >=6.5%.
Fasting plasma glucose: normal <100 mg/dL; prediabetes 100-125; diabetes >=126.
2-hour 75-g OGTT: normal <140 mg/dL; prediabetes 140-199; diabetes >=200.
Random plasma glucose >=200 mg/dL with classic symptoms supports diabetes diagnosis.
DKA: diabetes/hyperglycemia plus ketosis plus metabolic acidosis; glucose may be lower in euglycemic DKA.
DKA beta-hydroxybutyrate often >=3.0 mmol/L with pH <7.3 or bicarbonate <18 mmol/L.
Use local laboratory intervals and assay-specific cutoffs; endocrine immunoassays are affected by pregnancy, age, sex, acute illness, binding proteins, biotin, and medication effects.
red_flags: Vomiting, dehydration, hypotension, Kussmaul respirations, altered mental status.
SGLT2 inhibitor with ketotic symptoms even if glucose is not very high.
Hemodynamic instability, altered mental status, severe electrolyte/glucose abnormality, arrhythmia, airway compromise, or vision threat requires urgent escalation.
DKA/HHS physiology, recurrent severe hypoglycemia, vomiting/dehydration, altered mental status, infected foot wound, or pregnancy with hyperglycemia.
management_changes: Ketosis plus acidosis requires urgent DKA pathway.
Positive antibodies or low C-peptide supports autoimmune insulin-deficient diabetes.
Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care.
Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment.
Ketosis/acidosis, HHS osmolality, or severe hypoglycemia triggers urgent protocolized treatment rather than routine outpatient adjustment.
CKD, albuminuria, ASCVD/HF risk, pregnancy, foot infection, or insulin access barriers change medication choice and follow-up urgency.
decision_steps: If symptomatic hyperglycemia, check glucose, beta-hydroxybutyrate or urine ketones, electrolytes, anion gap, bicarbonate, pH, and osmolality as indicated.
Confirm autoimmune insulin-deficient diabetes with islet autoantibodies and C-peptide interpreted with concurrent glucose when presentation is not classic.
Classify acute DKA/HHS severity before routine chronic diabetes planning; euglycemic DKA remains possible with SGLT2 inhibitor exposure.
After stabilization, screen thyroid/celiac/autoimmune comorbidity and chronic complications according to duration and age.
Confirm that Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus is the selected clinical intent, then screen for red flags before routine outpatient testing.

Order the first-line tests in sequence: A1c/glucose criteria plus ketones/acid-base if symptomatic; Beta-hydroxybutyrate, BMP, venous/arterial pH, bicarbonate, anion gap; Islet autoantibodies: GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, insulin Ab if needed; C-peptide with concurrent glucose.

Interpret numeric results against the workup cutoffs and local assay ranges: A1c: normal <5.7%; prediabetes 5.7-6.4%; diabetes >=6.5%. Fasting plasma glucose: normal <100 mg/dL; prediabetes 100-125; diabetes >=126.

If the initial result is equivocal, discordant with the phenotype, or affected by medication, acute illness, pregnancy, age, sex, or assay interference, repeat or confirm using the next guideline test before committing to long-term therapy.
treatment_options: Treat DKA/HHS with institutional protocol for IV fluids, insulin, potassium, dextrose transition, precipitant treatment, and monitored disposition.
Use lifelong physiologic basal-bolus insulin or pump therapy with CGM when feasible, plus education on carbohydrate dosing and correction factors.
Prescribe glucagon rescue, sick-day rules, ketone testing, backup basal insulin plan, and supplies before discharge or transition.
Adjust regimen to avoid severe hypoglycemia and address technology access, exercise, meals, pregnancy, renal function, and psychosocial barriers.
Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus; draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization.
For confirmed Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus, choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway.
Monitor treatment response with the condition-specific clinical endpoint plus the most relevant labs or imaging listed in this workup.
Escalate to endocrinology or the relevant specialty when results are severe, discordant, recurrent, treatment-limiting, or require localization, surgery, genetic testing, or high-risk medication.

basic_bedside_data_safety_checks: Measure blood pressure: Check and document as baseline bedside data for this workup. Hypotension or severe hypertension changes acuity, medication safety, fluid strategy, and escalation. Rationale: Targets hemodynamic safety, hypertension or hypotension severity, and medication or fluid-safety context; positive, negative, or unable-to-assess findings guide escalation, diagnostic testing, treatment safety, or specialty planning for Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Measure heart rate: Check and document as baseline bedside data for this workup. Tachycardia, bradycardia, or irregular rhythm changes acuity, ECG need, and treatment urgency. Rationale: Screens severity and immediate safety findings that can change triage, monitoring, fluids, or urgent escalation for Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Measure current weight: Check and document as baseline bedside data for this workup. Weight change influences insulin resistance framing, nutrition plan, and medication selection. Rationale: Quantifies anthropometric and cardiometabolic risk so abnormal values can change diagnostic criteria, treatment selection, dosing context, or prevention intensity for Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.
clinical_questions: How much are you drinking and urinating, including polyuria, polydipsia, overnight urination, thirst intensity, access to water, and any dehydration or confusion? Purpose: Defines symptom severity, tempo, complications, and whether Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus needs urgent evaluation today. Management: A positive answer changes urgency from routine diabetes assessment to same-day hyperglycemia, ketosis, dehydration, infection, or insulin-delivery evaluation for Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

How has weight changed from baseline, and was the change intentional or associated with appetite, fluid status, or systemic symptoms? Any foot ulcer, wound, skin redness, drainage, tenderness, indwelling line/device, or procedure-site concern suggesting infection? core_physical_exam_maneuvers: Inspect feet for ulcers: Inspect both feet including interdigital spaces and pressure points for ulcer, callus, erythema, drainage, deformity, or infection. Findings: No ulcer / Ulcer/wound / Callus/deformity / Infection concern / Unable to assess. Target: diabetic neuropathy, foot ulcer risk, infection, or peripheral arterial disease Management: An ulcer or wound changes infection workup, offloading, antibiotic planning, and disposition. Source: ADA_SOC_2026; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Palpate pedal pulses: Palpate bilateral pulses at the named sites, comparing amplitude, symmetry, rhythm, and perfusion. Findings: Symmetric/normal / Diminished / Absent / Asymmetric / Unable to assess. Target: diabetic foot perfusion, peripheral arterial disease, or vascular risk Management: Diminished pulses change vascular risk assessment and foot-wound management. Source: ADA_SOC_2026; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Test monofilament sensation: Apply a 10-g monofilament perpendicular to standard plantar sites until it bends, with eyes closed, documenting sites felt or missed. Findings: Protective sensation intact / Reduced / Absent / Unable to assess. Target: diabetic neuropathy, foot ulcer risk, infection, or peripheral arterial disease Management: Loss of protective sensation changes foot-care counseling, footwear planning, and ulcer prevention. Source: ADA_SOC_2026; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment 10-g monofilament
conditional_exam_add_ons: Observe Kussmaul breathing: Observe respiratory rate, depth, accessory muscle use, speech tolerance, and breathing pattern at rest. Findings: Absent / Deep/labored pattern / Respiratory distress / Unable to assess. Target: respiratory compensation, pulmonary congestion, infection, obstruction, or distress Management: Kussmaul breathing changes crisis severity and urgent treatment pathway. Source: ADA_SOC_2026; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Palpate abdomen for tenderness: Inspect and palpate the abdomen gently, localizing tenderness and stopping if guarding, rebound, or severe pain occurs. Findings: Nontender / Tender / Guarding / Rebound/peritoneal concern / Unable to assess. Target: abdominal complication, endocrine crisis mimic, or precipitating illness Management: Tenderness changes precipitant evaluation and concern for surgical or pancreatitis mimic. Source: ADA_SOC_2026; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect insulin pump site: Inspect the infusion or device site for dislodgement, leakage, erythema, tenderness, occlusion, or supply failure. Findings: Site intact / Dislodged/occluded/leaking / Erythema/tenderness/drainage / Unable to assess. Target: insulin delivery failure, infusion-site infection, or device-related precipitant Management: Site failure changes precipitant assessment and insulin delivery plan. Source: ADA_SOC_2026; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect foot wound: Inspect both feet including interdigital spaces and pressure points for ulcer, callus, erythema, drainage, deformity, or infection. Findings: No ulcer / Ulcer/wound / Callus/deformity / Infection concern / Unable to assess. Target: diabetic neuropathy, foot ulcer risk, infection, or peripheral arterial disease Management: A wound changes infection workup, antibiotics, and disposition. Source: ADA_SOC_2026; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect mucous membranes for dehydration: Inspect oral mucosa and tongue moisture with attention to dry mucosa, cracked lips, and overall hydration context. Findings: Moist / Dry / Very dry / Unable to assess. Target: volume status, dehydration severity, or peripheral perfusion Management: Dehydration changes fluid strategy, lab urgency, and monitoring needs. Source: ADA_SOC_2026; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

clinical_decision_tree: If symptomatic hyperglycemia, check glucose, beta-hydroxybutyrate or urine ketones, electrolytes, anion gap, bicarbonate, pH, and osmolality as indicated. Rationale: Routes Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Confirm autoimmune insulin-deficient diabetes with islet autoantibodies and C-peptide interpreted with concurrent glucose when presentation is not classic. Rationale: Routes Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ADA_DIAGNOSIS_2026.

Classify acute DKA/HHS severity before routine chronic diabetes planning; euglycemic DKA remains possible with SGLT2 inhibitor exposure. Rationale: Routes Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ADA_HYPERGLYCEMIC_CRISES_2024.

After stabilization, screen thyroid/celiac/autoimmune comorbidity and chronic complications according to duration and age. Rationale: Routes Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Confirm that Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus is the selected clinical intent, then screen for red flags before routine outpatient testing. Rationale: Routes Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ADA_DIAGNOSIS_2026.

Order the first-line tests in sequence: A1c/glucose criteria plus ketones/acid-base if symptomatic; Beta-hydroxybutyrate, BMP, venous/arterial pH, bicarbonate, anion gap; Islet autoantibodies: GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, insulin Ab if needed; C-peptide with concurrent glucose. Rationale: Routes Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ADA_HYPERGLYCEMIC_CRISES_2024.

Fasting plasma glucose: normal <100 mg/dL; prediabetes 100-125; diabetes >=126. Rationale: Routes Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

If the initial result is equivocal, discordant with the phenotype, or affected by medication, acute illness, pregnancy, age, sex, or assay interference, repeat or confirm using the next guideline test before committing to long-term therapy. Rationale: Routes Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ADA_DIAGNOSIS_2026.
red_flags: Vomiting, dehydration, hypotension, Kussmaul respirations, altered mental status.: Escalate urgently, reassess severity, and evaluate for dangerous mimics or complications. Rationale: Identifies dangerous Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus presentations that should override routine outpatient workup and prompt urgent escalation. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

SGLT2 inhibitor with ketotic symptoms even if glucose is not very high.: Escalate urgently, reassess severity, and evaluate for dangerous mimics or complications. Rationale: Identifies dangerous Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus presentations that should override routine outpatient workup and prompt urgent escalation. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Hemodynamic instability, altered mental status, severe electrolyte/glucose abnormality, arrhythmia, airway compromise, or vision threat requires urgent escalation.: Escalate urgently, reassess severity, and evaluate for dangerous mimics or complications. Rationale: Identifies dangerous Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus presentations that should override routine outpatient workup and prompt urgent escalation. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

DKA/HHS physiology, recurrent severe hypoglycemia, vomiting/dehydration, altered mental status, infected foot wound, or pregnancy with hyperglycemia.: Escalate urgently, reassess severity, and evaluate for dangerous mimics or complications. Rationale: Identifies dangerous Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus presentations that should override routine outpatient workup and prompt urgent escalation. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.
results_that_change_management: Ketosis plus acidosis requires urgent DKA pathway. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Positive antibodies or low C-peptide supports autoimmune insulin-deficient diabetes. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_DIAGNOSIS_2026.

Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_HYPERGLYCEMIC_CRISES_2024.

Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Ketosis/acidosis, HHS osmolality, or severe hypoglycemia triggers urgent protocolized treatment rather than routine outpatient adjustment. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_DIAGNOSIS_2026.

CKD, albuminuria, ASCVD/HF risk, pregnancy, foot infection, or insulin access barriers change medication choice and follow-up urgency. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus. Source: ADA_HYPERGLYCEMIC_CRISES_2024.
treatment_options: Treat DKA/HHS with institutional protocol for IV fluids, insulin, potassium, dextrose transition, precipitant treatment, and monitored disposition. Rationale: Links confirmed Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Use lifelong physiologic basal-bolus insulin or pump therapy with CGM when feasible, plus education on carbohydrate dosing and correction factors. Rationale: Links confirmed Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ADA_DIAGNOSIS_2026.

Prescribe glucagon rescue, sick-day rules, ketone testing, backup basal insulin plan, and supplies before discharge or transition. Rationale: Links confirmed Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ADA_HYPERGLYCEMIC_CRISES_2024.

Adjust regimen to avoid severe hypoglycemia and address technology access, exercise, meals, pregnancy, renal function, and psychosocial barriers. Rationale: Links confirmed Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus; draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization. Rationale: Links confirmed Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ADA_DIAGNOSIS_2026.

For confirmed Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus, choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway. Rationale: Links confirmed Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ADA_HYPERGLYCEMIC_CRISES_2024.

Monitor treatment response with the condition-specific clinical endpoint plus the most relevant labs or imaging listed in this workup. Rationale: Links confirmed Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Escalate to endocrinology or the relevant specialty when results are severe, discordant, recurrent, treatment-limiting, or require localization, surgery, genetic testing, or high-risk medication. Rationale: Links confirmed Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ADA_DIAGNOSIS_2026.

questions: Weight, activity, family history, prior GDM.
Sleep apnea, fatty liver, PCOS, steroid exposure.
What is the current trajectory, baseline status, prior diagnosis date, and what changed today?
Which medications, supplements, missed doses, recent procedures, acute illnesses, pregnancy/fertility context, or assay-interfering factors could change interpretation?
Ask about weight trajectory, activity, nutrition, sleep apnea, fatty liver, PCOS, prior gestational diabetes, family diabetes/ASCVD history, steroid exposure, and cardiometabolic medications.
Clarify hypertension, dyslipidemia, ASCVD/HF/CKD risk, smoking, pregnancy possibility, and barriers to prevention because these change cardiometabolic risk reduction.
exam: BP, BMI/waist.
Acanthosis nigricans and cardiometabolic exam.
Vitals and acuity screen: BP, HR, respiratory status, temperature when acute, weight/BMI or waist when cardiometabolic risk matters.
Targeted complication screen guided by symptoms: volume/perfusion, mental status, skin/mucosa, neuromuscular findings, and organ-system complications.
Acanthosis nigricans and anthropometric cardiometabolic phenotype when insulin resistance is suspected.
Cardiovascular risk exam only when symptoms, hypertension, established ASCVD, or heart failure features are present.
tests: A1c, fasting plasma glucose, or 2-hour 75-g OGTT.
Lipids, ALT if fatty liver risk, BP/BMI/waist.
Compare with prior results and repeat discordant nonurgent endocrine tests using correct timing, local assay, and interference precautions.

Cardiometabolic safety data: confirm A1c/FPG/OGTT classification, document BP/waist/BMI, and check lipids, kidney function, albuminuria when diabetes-range, and liver/fatty-liver context when treatment selection or risk reduction is in scope.
A1c or plasma glucose criteria for prediabetes/diabetes classification, with repeat confirmatory testing when asymptomatic or discordant.
Lipids, blood pressure, kidney function, albuminuria when diabetes-range, and liver context when cardiometabolic risk reduction or medication choice is in scope.
reference_values: A1c: normal <5.7%; prediabetes 5.7-6.4%; diabetes >=6.5%.

2-hour 75-g OGTT: normal <140 mg/dL; prediabetes 140-199; diabetes >=200.
Random plasma glucose >=200 mg/dL with classic symptoms supports diabetes diagnosis.
Use local laboratory intervals and assay-specific cutoffs; endocrine immunoassays are affected by pregnancy, age, sex, acute illness, binding proteins, biotin, and medication effects.
red_flags: Diabetes-range glycemia or hyperglycemic symptoms.
Hemodynamic instability, altered mental status, severe electrolyte/glucose abnormality, arrhythmia, airway compromise, or vision threat requires urgent escalation.
Diabetes-range glycemia with classic symptoms, severe hypertension, ASCVD symptoms, pregnancy-context hyperglycemia, or dehydration requires escalation beyond routine prevention care.
management_changes: A1c 5.7-6.4, FPG 100-125, or 2-hour OGTT 140-199 triggers prevention and surveillance.
Diabetes-range values change diagnosis.
Markedly abnormal glucose, severe hypertension, pregnancy-context concern, or cardiopulmonary symptoms changes disposition from prevention visit to urgent evaluation.
Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment.
Prediabetes or metabolic syndrome thresholds change prevention intensity, lifestyle intervention, cardiometabolic risk-factor treatment, and surveillance interval.
Diabetes-range glycemia, ASCVD/HF/CKD risk, fatty liver, obstructive sleep apnea, PCOS, or pregnancy context changes diagnostic classification, treatment safety, and referral or follow-up urgency.
decision_steps: Classify prediabetes with A1c 5.7-6.4%, fasting glucose 100-125 mg/dL, or 2-hour OGTT 140-199 mg/dL after excluding diabetes-range results.
Assess BMI, waist, BP, lipids, fatty liver, sleep apnea, PCOS, prior gestational diabetes, medications, and ASCVD risk.
Repeat diabetes testing on a surveillance interval and sooner if symptoms, steroid exposure, pregnancy, or rising risk appears.
If any confirmatory result reaches diabetes range, switch to the diabetes diagnostic and treatment pathway.
Confirm that Prediabetes is the selected clinical intent, then screen for red flags before routine outpatient testing.
Order the first-line tests in sequence: A1c, fasting plasma glucose, or 2-hour 75-g OGTT; Lipids, ALT if fatty liver risk, BP/BMI/waist.

Interpret numeric results against the workup cutoffs and local assay ranges: A1c: normal <5.7%; prediabetes 5.7-6.4%; diabetes >=6.5%. Fasting plasma glucose: normal <100 mg/dL; prediabetes 100-125; diabetes >=126.

If the initial result is equivocal, discordant with the phenotype, or affected by medication, acute illness, pregnancy, age, sex, or assay interference, repeat or confirm using the next guideline test before committing to long-term therapy.
treatment_options: Offer intensive lifestyle intervention aiming for durable weight loss when appropriate, nutrition quality, physical activity, sleep, and tobacco risk reduction.
Consider metformin for higher-risk adults such as younger age with obesity, prior gestational diabetes, rising A1c, or other strong diabetes-risk features.
Treat BP, lipids, fatty liver risk, sleep apnea, and weight-related comorbidities as part of cardiometabolic risk reduction.
Use shared decision-making for anti-obesity pharmacotherapy or bariatric referral when BMI/comorbidity criteria and patient goals support it.
Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Prediabetes; draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization.
For confirmed Prediabetes, choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway.
Monitor treatment response with the condition-specific clinical endpoint plus the most relevant labs or imaging listed in this workup.
Escalate to endocrinology or the relevant specialty when results are severe, discordant, recurrent, treatment-limiting, or require localization, surgery, genetic testing, or high-risk medication.

Measure heart rate: Check and document as baseline bedside data for this workup. Tachycardia, bradycardia, or irregular rhythm changes acuity, ECG need, and treatment urgency. Measure current weight: Check and document as baseline bedside data for this workup. Weight change influences insulin resistance framing, nutrition plan, and medication selection. results_that_change_management: A1c 5.7-6.4, FPG 100-125, or 2-hour OGTT 140-199 triggers prevention and surveillance. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Prediabetes. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.
Diabetes-range values change diagnosis. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Prediabetes. Source: ADA_DIAGNOSIS_2026.

treatment_options: Offer intensive lifestyle intervention aiming for durable weight loss when appropriate, nutrition quality, physical activity, sleep, and tobacco risk reduction. Consider metformin for higher-risk adults such as younger age with obesity, prior gestational diabetes, rising A1c, or other strong diabetes-risk features. questions: Gestational age, prior GDM/macrosomia.
Preexisting diabetes risk and fetal growth/polyhydramnios if known.
What is the current trajectory, baseline status, prior diagnosis date, and what changed today?
Which medications, supplements, missed doses, recent procedures, acute illnesses, pregnancy/fertility context, or assay-interfering factors could change interpretation?

Ask about gestational age, prior gestational diabetes or macrosomia, home glucose pattern if available, vomiting/dehydration, ketone symptoms, fetal growth/polyhydramnios context, and obstetric care plan.
Clarify preexisting diabetes risk, medications, nutrition access, steroid exposure, hypertension or preeclampsia symptoms, and postpartum follow-up barriers.
exam: BP and weight.
Volume status if vomiting/hyperglycemia.
Pregnancy-focused assessment with OB team.
Vitals and acuity screen: BP, HR, respiratory status, temperature when acute, weight/BMI or waist when cardiometabolic risk matters.
Targeted complication screen guided by symptoms: volume/perfusion, mental status, skin/mucosa, neuromuscular findings, and organ-system complications.
Volume status, mucous membranes, perfusion, and mental status when vomiting, severe hyperglycemia, ketones, or dehydration are possible.
Pregnancy and hypertension-focused assessment with the obstetric team when blood pressure symptoms, preeclampsia features, or fetal-growth concern exists.
tests: Early risk testing if high risk.
24-28 week one-step 75-g OGTT or local two-step strategy.
Postpartum 75-g OGTT at 4-12 weeks.
Compare with prior results and repeat discordant nonurgent endocrine tests using correct timing, local assay, and interference precautions.

Gestational diabetes safety tests: route the local OGTT strategy with gestational age, review home glucose pattern if available, and check ketones, electrolytes/anion gap, creatinine, and acid-base status for vomiting, dehydration, severe hyperglycemia, or insulin-deficiency symptoms.
Gestational diabetes screening/diagnostic OGTT using the local one-step or two-step strategy and gestational-age context.
Glucose monitoring plan; ketones, electrolytes, renal function, and acid-base assessment when vomiting, dehydration, severe hyperglycemia, or insulin deficiency is possible.
reference_values: One-step 75-g OGTT for GDM: fasting >=92, 1-hour >=180, or 2-hour >=153 mg/dL.
Two-step approach if used: 50-g screen often >=130-140 mg/dL; 100-g Carpenter-Coustan thresholds fasting 95, 1-hour 180, 2-hour 155, 3-hour 140 mg/dL, usually 2 abnormal values.
A1c: normal <5.7%; prediabetes 5.7-6.4%; diabetes >=6.5%.

2-hour 75-g OGTT: normal <140 mg/dL; prediabetes 140-199; diabetes >=200.
Random plasma glucose >=200 mg/dL with classic symptoms supports diabetes diagnosis.
Use local laboratory intervals and assay-specific cutoffs; endocrine immunoassays are affected by pregnancy, age, sex, acute illness, binding proteins, biotin, and medication effects.
red_flags: Ketones, vomiting/dehydration, severe hyperglycemia.
Hypertension or preeclampsia features.
Hemodynamic instability, altered mental status, severe electrolyte/glucose abnormality, arrhythmia, airway compromise, or vision threat requires urgent escalation.
Ketones, vomiting/dehydration, severe hyperglycemia, altered mental status, hypertension/preeclampsia features, or reduced fetal movement requires urgent obstetric/endocrine evaluation.
management_changes: OGTT threshold crossing changes maternal/fetal monitoring and therapy.
Postpartum abnormal OGTT changes long-term prevention.
Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care.
Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment.
OGTT threshold crossing changes maternal-fetal monitoring, nutrition therapy, glucose monitoring, medication escalation, and delivery/postpartum planning.
Ketones, vomiting/dehydration, severe hyperglycemia, altered mental status, or hypertensive features in pregnancy changes disposition to urgent obstetric/endocrine evaluation.
decision_steps: Confirm pregnancy status and gestational age; screen high-risk patients early for overt diabetes and otherwise test at 24-28 weeks using the local one-step or two-step strategy.
Interpret one-step 75-g OGTT using fasting >=92, 1-hour >=180, or 2-hour >=153 mg/dL; one abnormal value diagnoses GDM in that strategy.
If using two-step testing, route abnormal 50-g screen to diagnostic 100-g OGTT and apply local Carpenter-Coustan or NDDG thresholds.
After delivery, schedule 75-g OGTT at 4-12 weeks and long-term diabetes surveillance.
Confirm that Gestational Diabetes is the selected clinical intent, then screen for red flags before routine outpatient testing.
Order the first-line tests in sequence: Early risk testing if high risk; 24-28 week one-step 75-g OGTT or local two-step strategy; Postpartum 75-g OGTT at 4-12 weeks.

Interpret numeric results against the workup cutoffs and local assay ranges: One-step 75-g OGTT for GDM: fasting >=92, 1-hour >=180, or 2-hour >=153 mg/dL. Two-step approach if used: 50-g screen often >=130-140 mg/dL; 100-g Carpenter-Coustan thresholds fasting 95, 1-hour 180, 2-hour 155, 3-hour 140 mg/dL, usually 2 abnormal values.

If the initial result is equivocal, discordant with the phenotype, or affected by medication, acute illness, pregnancy, age, sex, or assay interference, repeat or confirm using the next guideline test before committing to long-term therapy.
treatment_options: Start nutrition therapy, activity as obstetrically appropriate, glucose monitoring, and obstetric fetal-growth coordination.
Add insulin when lifestyle therapy does not meet pregnancy glucose targets or if fasting/postprandial values are persistently above local goals.
Use metformin or glyburide only within local obstetric/endocrine policy and shared decision-making about placental transfer and failure rates.
Escalate vomiting, ketones, dehydration, severe hyperglycemia, hypertension, or fetal/maternal danger signs urgently.
Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Gestational Diabetes; draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization.
For confirmed Gestational Diabetes, choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway.
Monitor treatment response with the condition-specific clinical endpoint plus the most relevant labs or imaging listed in this workup.
Escalate to endocrinology or the relevant specialty when results are severe, discordant, recurrent, treatment-limiting, or require localization, surgery, genetic testing, or high-risk medication.

core_physical_exam_maneuvers: Inspect neck for acanthosis: Inspect the named area directly, comparing sides when relevant and documenting presence, absence, severity, and inability to assess. clinical_decision_tree: Confirm pregnancy status and gestational age; screen high-risk patients early for overt diabetes and otherwise test at 24-28 weeks using the local one-step or two-step strategy. Rationale: Routes Gestational Diabetes testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

If using two-step testing, route abnormal 50-g screen to diagnostic 100-g OGTT and apply local Carpenter-Coustan or NDDG thresholds. Rationale: Routes Gestational Diabetes testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

After delivery, schedule 75-g OGTT at 4-12 weeks and long-term diabetes surveillance. Rationale: Routes Gestational Diabetes testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ADA_DIAGNOSIS_2026.

treatment_options: Start nutrition therapy, activity as obstetrically appropriate, glucose monitoring, and obstetric fetal-growth coordination. Rationale: Routes Metabolic Syndrome testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: NHLBI_METABOLIC_SYNDROME.

Order the first-line tests in sequence: Waist circumference; Fasting triglycerides, HDL, fasting glucose/A1c; Blood pressure and medication review. Rationale: Routes Metabolic Syndrome testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Interpret numeric results against the workup cutoffs and local assay ranges: Diagnosis commonly requires 3 or more: waist >102 cm men or >88 cm women in ATP III US cut points, TG >=150, HDL <40 men/<50 women, BP >=130/85 or treatment, fasting glucose >=100 or treatment. Rationale: Routes Metabolic Syndrome testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: NHLBI_METABOLIC_SYNDROME.

If the initial result is equivocal, discordant with the phenotype, or affected by medication, acute illness, pregnancy, age, sex, or assay interference, repeat or confirm using the next guideline test before committing to long-term therapy. Rationale: Routes Metabolic Syndrome testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.
red_flags: Diabetes-range glycemia, severe hypertension, ASCVD symptoms.: Escalate urgently, reassess severity, and evaluate for dangerous mimics or complications. Rationale: Identifies dangerous Metabolic Syndrome presentations that should override routine outpatient workup and prompt urgent escalation. Source: NHLBI_METABOLIC_SYNDROME.

Hemodynamic instability, altered mental status, severe electrolyte/glucose abnormality, arrhythmia, airway compromise, or vision threat requires urgent escalation.: Escalate urgently, reassess severity, and evaluate for dangerous mimics or complications. Rationale: Identifies dangerous Metabolic Syndrome presentations that should override routine outpatient workup and prompt urgent escalation. Source: NHLBI_METABOLIC_SYNDROME.

Diabetes-range glycemia with classic symptoms, severe hypertension, ASCVD symptoms, pregnancy-context hyperglycemia, or dehydration requires escalation beyond routine prevention care.: Escalate urgently, reassess severity, and evaluate for dangerous mimics or complications. Rationale: Identifies dangerous Metabolic Syndrome presentations that should override routine outpatient workup and prompt urgent escalation. Source: NHLBI_METABOLIC_SYNDROME.
results_that_change_management: 3 or more criteria shifts to intensive cardiometabolic risk reduction. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Metabolic Syndrome. Source: NHLBI_METABOLIC_SYNDROME.
Diabetes thresholds change diagnosis. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Metabolic Syndrome. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Markedly abnormal glucose, severe hypertension, pregnancy-context concern, or cardiopulmonary symptoms changes disposition from prevention visit to urgent evaluation. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Metabolic Syndrome. Source: NHLBI_METABOLIC_SYNDROME.

Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Metabolic Syndrome. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Prediabetes or metabolic syndrome thresholds change prevention intensity, lifestyle intervention, cardiometabolic risk-factor treatment, and surveillance interval. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Metabolic Syndrome. Source: NHLBI_METABOLIC_SYNDROME.

Diabetes-range glycemia, ASCVD/HF/CKD risk, fatty liver, obstructive sleep apnea, PCOS, or pregnancy context changes diagnostic classification, treatment safety, and referral or follow-up urgency. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Metabolic Syndrome. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.
treatment_options: Use intensive lifestyle intervention for weight, waist, diet quality, physical activity, sleep, and alcohol/tobacco risk. Rationale: Links confirmed Metabolic Syndrome results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: NHLBI_METABOLIC_SYNDROME.

Treat hypertension, dyslipidemia, hypertriglyceridemia, and diabetes/prediabetes according to their individual guideline thresholds. Rationale: Links confirmed Metabolic Syndrome results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Consider anti-obesity medication or bariatric referral when BMI criteria and comorbidity burden support it. Rationale: Links confirmed Metabolic Syndrome results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: NHLBI_METABOLIC_SYNDROME.

Coordinate fatty liver, sleep apnea, PCOS, CKD, and ASCVD prevention follow-up. Rationale: Links confirmed Metabolic Syndrome results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Metabolic Syndrome; draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization.
