For confirmed Metabolic Syndrome, choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway. Rationale: Links confirmed Metabolic Syndrome results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

Monitor treatment response with the condition-specific clinical endpoint plus the most relevant labs or imaging listed in this workup. Rationale: Links confirmed Metabolic Syndrome results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: NHLBI_METABOLIC_SYNDROME.

Escalate to endocrinology or the relevant specialty when results are severe, discordant, recurrent, treatment-limiting, or require localization, surgery, genetic testing, or high-risk medication. Rationale: Links confirmed Metabolic Syndrome results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ADA_SOC_2026.

questions: Fatigue, cold intolerance, constipation, weight gain, depression, menorrhagia.
Amiodarone, lithium, immune checkpoint inhibitor, pregnancy/fertility context.
What is the current trajectory, baseline status, prior diagnosis date, and what changed today?
Which medications, supplements, missed doses, recent procedures, acute illnesses, pregnancy/fertility context, or assay-interfering factors could change interpretation?
Ask about palpitations, heat/cold intolerance, weight change, bowel change, tremor, fatigue, mood/cognition, menstrual/fertility context, and neck symptoms.
Ask about amiodarone, lithium, iodine/contrast, immune checkpoint inhibitors, thyroid hormone or antithyroid drugs, biotin, pregnancy/postpartum status, radiation, and family thyroid cancer/MEN2.
exam: HR/BP, thyroid size/nodules.
Delayed reflexes, edema, skin/hair.
Mental status if severe.
Vitals and acuity screen: BP, HR, respiratory status, temperature when acute, weight/BMI or waist when cardiometabolic risk matters.
Targeted complication screen guided by symptoms: volume/perfusion, mental status, skin/mucosa, neuromuscular findings, and organ-system complications.
Thyroid/neck exam for goiter, nodules, tenderness, asymmetry, bruit, fixation, compressive symptoms, and cervical/supraclavicular nodes when structural concern exists.
Cardiac rhythm/perfusion, tremor, reflex relaxation, skin/hair, edema, and eye/orbitopathy screen when hypo- or hyperthyroid features are present.
tests: TSH and free T4.
TPO antibody for autoimmune etiology.
Pregnancy test when relevant; pituitary/adrenal evaluation if central pattern.
Compare with prior results and repeat discordant nonurgent endocrine tests using correct timing, local assay, and interference precautions.

Hypothyroid safety data: check TSH/free T4 with TPO antibody when autoimmune disease matters, sodium/CK/lipids if severe or symptomatic, ECG/cardiac context for older or cardiac patients, and pregnancy-specific targets when pregnant or fertility planning.
TSH with free T4; add T3 for thyrotoxicosis, TPO Ab for autoimmune hypothyroidism, TRAb/TSI for Graves, and thyroid ultrasound/FNA pathway for nodules.
Hold high-dose biotin before susceptible assays when feasible and interpret thyroid labs with pregnancy, acute illness, and central hypothyroidism context.
reference_values: TSH typical adult range about 0.4-4.0 mIU/L; use local lab, pregnancy, and illness context.
Free T4 typical adult range about 0.8-1.8 ng/dL; use local assay.
T3, TRAb/TSI, TPO Ab, and thyroglobulin Ab are assay-specific.
Use local laboratory intervals and assay-specific cutoffs; endocrine immunoassays are affected by pregnancy, age, sex, acute illness, binding proteins, biotin, and medication effects.
red_flags: Myxedema coma features: hypothermia, bradycardia, hypotension, hypoventilation, hyponatremia, altered mental status.
Hemodynamic instability, altered mental status, severe electrolyte/glucose abnormality, arrhythmia, airway compromise, or vision threat requires urgent escalation.
Thyroid storm, myxedema coma, atrial fibrillation/heart failure, vision-threatening orbitopathy, rapidly enlarging neck mass, hoarseness, dysphagia, dyspnea, or suspicious nodes.
management_changes: High TSH plus low FT4 confirms overt primary hypothyroidism.
Low FT4 with low/normal TSH triggers central hypothyroidism/pituitary pathway.
Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care.
Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment.
Suppressed TSH with elevated T4/T3 changes to thyrotoxicosis severity and etiology workup; low FT4 with low/normal TSH triggers central hypothyroidism evaluation.
Suspicious nodule, compressive symptom, abnormal node, calcitonin/RET concern, or high-risk ultrasound feature changes imaging/FNA/surgical referral pathway.
decision_steps: Start with TSH and free T4; high TSH with low free T4 supports overt primary hypothyroidism, while low free T4 with low/normal TSH suggests central disease.
Check TPO antibody when autoimmune thyroiditis changes prognosis, pregnancy planning, or etiology; ultrasound is reserved for goiter, nodule, asymmetry, or compressive symptoms.
Before levothyroxine escalation in pituitary disease or severe illness, assess adrenal insufficiency risk.
Screen urgently for myxedema coma physiology when hypothermia, bradycardia, hypotension, hypoventilation, hyponatremia, or altered mental status is present.
Confirm that Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid) is the selected clinical intent, then screen for red flags before routine outpatient testing.
Order the first-line tests in sequence: TSH and free T4; TPO antibody for autoimmune etiology; Pregnancy test when relevant; pituitary/adrenal evaluation if central pattern.

Interpret numeric results against the workup cutoffs and local assay ranges: TSH typical adult range about 0.4-4.0 mIU/L; use local lab, pregnancy, and illness context. Free T4 typical adult range about 0.8-1.8 ng/dL; use local assay.

If the initial result is equivocal, discordant with the phenotype, or affected by medication, acute illness, pregnancy, age, sex, or assay interference, repeat or confirm using the next guideline test before committing to long-term therapy.
treatment_options: Treat overt primary hypothyroidism with levothyroxine, adjusted for age, cardiac disease, pregnancy, weight, adherence, and interacting medications.
For subclinical hypothyroidism, individualize treatment by TSH level, symptoms, antibodies, pregnancy/fertility context, goiter, and cardiovascular risk.
Use free T4 rather than TSH to titrate central hypothyroidism, after adrenal status is safe.
For suspected myxedema coma, use emergency IV thyroid hormone, stress-dose glucocorticoid coverage, warming, ventilation, sodium/glucose correction, and ICU-level care per local protocol.
Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid); draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization.
For confirmed Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid), choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway.
Monitor treatment response with the condition-specific clinical endpoint plus the most relevant labs or imaging listed in this workup.
Escalate to endocrinology or the relevant specialty when results are severe, discordant, recurrent, treatment-limiting, or require localization, surgery, genetic testing, or high-risk medication.

basic_bedside_data_safety_checks: Measure blood pressure: Check and document as baseline bedside data for this workup. Hypotension or severe hypertension changes acuity, medication safety, fluid strategy, and escalation. Rationale: Targets hemodynamic safety, hypertension or hypotension severity, and medication or fluid-safety context; positive, negative, or unable-to-assess findings guide escalation, diagnostic testing, treatment safety, or specialty planning for Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid). Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014.

Measure heart rate: Check and document as baseline bedside data for this workup. Tachycardia, bradycardia, or irregular rhythm changes acuity, ECG need, and treatment urgency. Rationale: Screens severity and immediate safety findings that can change triage, monitoring, fluids, or urgent escalation for Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid). Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014.
clinical_questions: Any fatigue, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, hoarse voice, slowed thinking, depression, heavy menses, or weight gain suggesting hypothyroidism? Options: Not applicable / Pregnancy possible / Pregnant/postpartum / Cycle irregularity / Fertility goal active / Timing known / Unknown / Other ___. Purpose: Defines symptom severity, tempo, complications, and whether Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid) needs urgent evaluation today. Management: A positive answer raises concern for thyroid hormone deficiency or myxedema physiology and changes TSH/free T4 interpretation, replacement planning, and emergency-risk screening for Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid). Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014.

How has weight changed from baseline, and was the change intentional or associated with appetite, fluid status, or systemic symptoms? Options: Today or acute onset / Days / Weeks to months / Chronic or baseline / Worse today / Unknown / Other ___. Purpose: Defines symptom severity, tempo, complications, and whether Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid) needs urgent evaluation today. Management: The answer changes cardiometabolic risk stratification, prevention intensity, complication screening, and follow-up interval for Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid). Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014.

Any thyroid hormone, antithyroid drug, amiodarone, lithium, iodine/contrast exposure, biotin, supplement use, missed dose, or recent dose change affecting thyroid interpretation? Options: No relevant exposure / Medication change / Supplement use / Missed doses / Steroid or hormone exposure / Biotin / Iodine/contrast exposure / Other ___. Purpose: Identifies exposures, physiologic states, or treatment changes that can explain abnormal results or make Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid) therapy unsafe. Management: Positive exposure changes whether to hold interfering agents, repeat testing, adjust treatment, or use a safer Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid) pathway. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014.

Any palpitations, tremor, heat intolerance, anxiety, insomnia, weight loss, diarrhea, or sweating suggesting thyroid hormone excess or adrenergic physiology? Options: No endocrine symptom / Heat intolerance/sweating / Cold intolerance/dry skin / Palpitations/tremor / Bowel change / Weight change / Other ___. Purpose: Defines symptom severity, tempo, complications, and whether Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid) needs urgent evaluation today. Management: A positive answer raises concern for thyroid hormone excess or thyroid storm and changes beta-blocker need, thyroid testing urgency, trigger search, and emergency endocrine escalation for Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid). Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014.

When was the last menstrual period, how regular are cycles, and has bleeding changed from baseline? Options: Today or acute onset / Days / Weeks to months / Chronic or baseline / Worse today / Unknown / Other ___. Purpose: Defines symptom severity, tempo, complications, and whether Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid) needs urgent evaluation today. Management: The answer changes the leading Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid) pathway by refining severity, mimics, confirmatory testing, treatment safety, and follow-up timing. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014.

Any neck swelling, thyroid pain or tenderness, pressure, hoarseness, trouble swallowing, positional shortness of breath, or rapidly enlarging neck mass? Options: No respiratory symptoms / Cough / Sputum / Shortness of breath / Pleuritic pain / Hypoxemia or oxygen need / Other ___. Purpose: Defines symptom severity, tempo, complications, and whether Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid) needs urgent evaluation today. Management: The answer changes thyroid ultrasound/FNA urgency, compressive-symptom escalation, cancer-risk framing, and endocrine or surgical referral timing for Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid). Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014.

Any childhood head/neck radiation, therapeutic radiation, occupational exposure, or family thyroid cancer risk factor? Options: No matching feature / Childhood head/neck radiation / Therapeutic radiation / Occupational exposure / Family thyroid cancer risk factor / Other ___. Purpose: Defines the patient-specific context needed to interpret endocrine tests, avoid common mimics, and choose the safest next step for Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid). Management: The answer changes thyroid ultrasound/FNA urgency, compressive-symptom escalation, cancer-risk framing, and endocrine or surgical referral timing for Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid). Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014.

Any personal or family history of MEN, VHL, NF1, SDHx-related tumors, medullary thyroid cancer, pheochromocytoma, paraganglioma, or adrenal/pituitary tumors? Options: No matching feature / Personal / Family history of MEN / VHL / NF1 / SDHx-related tumors / Medullary thyroid cancer / Pheochromocytoma / Paraganglioma / Other ___. Purpose: Defines the patient-specific context needed to interpret endocrine tests, avoid common mimics, and choose the safest next step for Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid). Management: The answer changes central endocrine localization, pituitary MRI or visual-field review, axis testing, and neurosurgical or ophthalmology escalation for Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid). Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014.
core_physical_exam_maneuvers: Palpate thyroid gland: Inspect and palpate the thyroid from in front or behind while the patient swallows, noting size, tenderness, asymmetry, nodules, fixation, or bruit if auscultated. Findings: Normal size / Goiter / Nodule/asymmetry / Tender / Fixed/hard / Unable to assess. Target: thyroid structural disease, compressive features, or thyroid inflammatory/autoimmune pattern Management: Goiter, tenderness, asymmetry, or a dominant nodule changes etiology and imaging decisions. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Palpate cervical lymph nodes: Palpate cervical and supraclavicular nodal chains systematically, noting size, tenderness, mobility, firmness, and laterality. Findings: No concerning nodes / Tender/mobile nodes / Firm/fixed nodes / Supraclavicular node / Unable to assess. Target: regional nodal disease or thyroid malignancy risk Management: Suspicious lymph nodes change ultrasound mapping, biopsy planning, and urgency. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Auscultate heart rhythm: Auscultate the precordium with diaphragm and bell as appropriate, noting rate regularity, extra sounds, murmurs, rubs, or gallop. Findings: Regular / Irregular / Tachycardic / Bradycardic / Unable to assess. Target: thyroid-related tachycardia, atrial fibrillation, bradycardia, or heart-failure complication Management: Irregular rhythm changes ECG need, beta-blocker consideration, and escalation. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment stethoscope

Observe outstretched-hands tremor: Ask the patient to extend both arms and spread fingers; observe for fine tremor and functional impact. Findings: No tremor / Fine tremor / Coarse tremor / Functionally limiting tremor / Unable to assess. Target: thyrotoxicosis adrenergic signs, medication effect, or endocrine-related neuromuscular irritability Management: Fine tremor supports adrenergic thyrotoxicosis and changes symptom-control decisions. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none
conditional_exam_add_ons: Inspect eyelid retraction: Inspect eyes and eyelids from the front and side for lid lag/retraction, proptosis, conjunctival injection, or exposure signs. Findings: Absent / Present / Asymmetric / Exposure concern / Unable to assess. Target: thyrotoxicosis adrenergic signs or Graves orbitopathy severity Management: Lid retraction supports Graves or adrenergic thyroid physiology and changes etiology assessment. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Test extraocular movements: Ask the patient to follow a target through cardinal gaze positions and observe for limitation, pain, diplopia, or nystagmus. Findings: Full / Restricted / Diplopia / Painful / Unable to assess. Target: Graves orbitopathy, diplopia, ocular motility restriction, or vision-threatening thyroid eye disease Management: Restriction changes orbitopathy severity and eye-care urgency. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Listen to voice quality: Listen to voice quality during normal speech and ask about new hoarseness, weak voice, or compressive symptoms. Findings: Normal voice / Hoarse / Weak/breathy / Stridor/airway concern / Unable to assess. Target: thyroid structural disease, compressive features, or thyroid inflammatory/autoimmune pattern Management: Hoarseness changes airway and malignancy concern. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect mucous membranes for dehydration: Inspect oral mucosa and tongue moisture with attention to dry mucosa, cracked lips, and overall hydration context. Findings: Moist / Dry / Very dry / Unable to assess. Target: volume status, dehydration severity, or peripheral perfusion Management: Dehydration changes fluid strategy, lab urgency, and monitoring needs. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Rationale: Routes Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid) testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. If the initial result is equivocal, discordant with the phenotype, or affected by medication, acute illness, pregnancy, age, sex, or assay interference, repeat or confirm using the next guideline test before committing to long-term therapy. red_flags: Myxedema coma features: hypothermia, bradycardia, hypotension, hypoventilation, hyponatremia, altered mental status.: Escalate urgently, reassess severity, and evaluate for dangerous mimics or complications. Rationale: Identifies dangerous Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid) presentations that should override routine outpatient workup and prompt urgent escalation. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014.

Hemodynamic instability, altered mental status, severe electrolyte/glucose abnormality, arrhythmia, airway compromise, or vision threat requires urgent escalation.: Escalate urgently, reassess severity, and evaluate for dangerous mimics or complications. Rationale: Identifies dangerous Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid) presentations that should override routine outpatient workup and prompt urgent escalation. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014.

Thyroid storm, myxedema coma, atrial fibrillation/heart failure, vision-threatening orbitopathy, rapidly enlarging neck mass, hoarseness, dysphagia, dyspnea, or suspicious nodes.: Escalate urgently, reassess severity, and evaluate for dangerous mimics or complications. Rationale: Identifies dangerous Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid) presentations that should override routine outpatient workup and prompt urgent escalation. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014.
results_that_change_management: High TSH plus low FT4 confirms overt primary hypothyroidism. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid). Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014.

Low FT4 with low/normal TSH triggers central hypothyroidism/pituitary pathway. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid). Source: AACE_ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2012.

Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid). Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014.

Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid). Source: AACE_ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2012.

Suppressed TSH with elevated T4/T3 changes to thyrotoxicosis severity and etiology workup; low FT4 with low/normal TSH triggers central hypothyroidism evaluation. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid). Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014.

Suspicious nodule, compressive symptom, abnormal node, calcitonin/RET concern, or high-risk ultrasound feature changes imaging/FNA/surgical referral pathway. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid). Source: AACE_ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2012.
treatment_options: Treat overt primary hypothyroidism with levothyroxine, adjusted for age, cardiac disease, pregnancy, weight, adherence, and interacting medications. Rationale: Links confirmed Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid) results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014.

For subclinical hypothyroidism, individualize treatment by TSH level, symptoms, antibodies, pregnancy/fertility context, goiter, and cardiovascular risk. Rationale: Links confirmed Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid) results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: AACE_ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2012.

Use free T4 rather than TSH to titrate central hypothyroidism, after adrenal status is safe. Rationale: Links confirmed Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid) results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014.

For suspected myxedema coma, use emergency IV thyroid hormone, stress-dose glucocorticoid coverage, warming, ventilation, sodium/glucose correction, and ICU-level care per local protocol. Rationale: Links confirmed Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid) results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: AACE_ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2012.

Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid); draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization. Rationale: Links confirmed Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid) results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014.

For confirmed Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid), choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway. Rationale: Links confirmed Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid) results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: AACE_ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2012.

Monitor treatment response with the condition-specific clinical endpoint plus the most relevant labs or imaging listed in this workup. Rationale: Links confirmed Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid) results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014.

Escalate to endocrinology or the relevant specialty when results are severe, discordant, recurrent, treatment-limiting, or require localization, surgery, genetic testing, or high-risk medication. Rationale: Links confirmed Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid) results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: AACE_ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2012.

questions: Hypothyroid symptoms, family autoimmune disease.
Neck swelling, nodules, compressive symptoms.
What is the current trajectory, baseline status, prior diagnosis date, and what changed today?
Which medications, supplements, missed doses, recent procedures, acute illnesses, pregnancy/fertility context, or assay-interfering factors could change interpretation?
Ask about palpitations, heat/cold intolerance, weight change, bowel change, tremor, fatigue, mood/cognition, menstrual/fertility context, and neck symptoms.
Ask about amiodarone, lithium, iodine/contrast, immune checkpoint inhibitors, thyroid hormone or antithyroid drugs, biotin, pregnancy/postpartum status, radiation, and family thyroid cancer/MEN2.
exam: Firm/rubbery goiter, nodules, cervical nodes.
Bradycardia, skin/reflex signs.
Vitals and acuity screen: BP, HR, respiratory status, temperature when acute, weight/BMI or waist when cardiometabolic risk matters.
Targeted complication screen guided by symptoms: volume/perfusion, mental status, skin/mucosa, neuromuscular findings, and organ-system complications.
Thyroid/neck exam for goiter, nodules, tenderness, asymmetry, bruit, fixation, compressive symptoms, and cervical/supraclavicular nodes when structural concern exists.
Cardiac rhythm/perfusion, tremor, reflex relaxation, skin/hair, edema, and eye/orbitopathy screen when hypo- or hyperthyroid features are present.
tests: TSH, free T4, TPO antibody.
Thyroglobulin antibody as adjunct.
Ultrasound only for nodule, goiter, or compressive symptoms.
Compare with prior results and repeat discordant nonurgent endocrine tests using correct timing, local assay, and interference precautions.

Hypothyroid safety data: check TSH/free T4 with TPO antibody when autoimmune disease matters, sodium/CK/lipids if severe or symptomatic, ECG/cardiac context for older or cardiac patients, and pregnancy-specific targets when pregnant or fertility planning.
TSH with free T4; add T3 for thyrotoxicosis, TPO Ab for autoimmune hypothyroidism, TRAb/TSI for Graves, and thyroid ultrasound/FNA pathway for nodules.
Hold high-dose biotin before susceptible assays when feasible and interpret thyroid labs with pregnancy, acute illness, and central hypothyroidism context.
reference_values: TSH typical adult range about 0.4-4.0 mIU/L; use local lab, pregnancy, and illness context.

Use local laboratory intervals and assay-specific cutoffs; endocrine immunoassays are affected by pregnancy, age, sex, acute illness, binding proteins, biotin, and medication effects.

Thyroid storm, myxedema coma, atrial fibrillation/heart failure, vision-threatening orbitopathy, rapidly enlarging neck mass, hoarseness, dysphagia, dyspnea, or suspicious nodes.

Suspicious nodule, compressive symptom, abnormal node, calcitonin/RET concern, or high-risk ultrasound feature changes imaging/FNA/surgical referral pathway.
decision_steps: Start with TSH and free T4; high TSH with low free T4 supports overt primary hypothyroidism, while low free T4 with low/normal TSH suggests central disease.
Check TPO antibody when autoimmune thyroiditis changes prognosis, pregnancy planning, or etiology; ultrasound is reserved for goiter, nodule, asymmetry, or compressive symptoms.
Before levothyroxine escalation in pituitary disease or severe illness, assess adrenal insufficiency risk.
Screen urgently for myxedema coma physiology when hypothermia, bradycardia, hypotension, hypoventilation, hyponatremia, or altered mental status is present.
Confirm that Hashimoto's Thyroiditis (Autoimmune Hypothyroidism) is the selected clinical intent, then screen for red flags before routine outpatient testing.
Order the first-line tests in sequence: TSH, free T4, TPO antibody; Thyroglobulin antibody as adjunct; Ultrasound only for nodule, goiter, or compressive symptoms.

If the initial result is equivocal, discordant with the phenotype, or affected by medication, acute illness, pregnancy, age, sex, or assay interference, repeat or confirm using the next guideline test before committing to long-term therapy.

Use free T4 rather than TSH to titrate central hypothyroidism, after adrenal status is safe.
For suspected myxedema coma, use emergency IV thyroid hormone, stress-dose glucocorticoid coverage, warming, ventilation, sodium/glucose correction, and ICU-level care per local protocol.
Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Hashimoto's Thyroiditis (Autoimmune Hypothyroidism); draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization.

For confirmed Hashimoto's Thyroiditis (Autoimmune Hypothyroidism), choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway.

basic_bedside_data_safety_checks: Measure blood pressure: Check and document as baseline bedside data for this workup. Hypotension or severe hypertension changes acuity, medication safety, fluid strategy, and escalation. Rationale: Targets hemodynamic safety, hypertension or hypotension severity, and medication or fluid-safety context; positive, negative, or unable-to-assess findings guide escalation, diagnostic testing, treatment safety, or specialty planning for Hashimoto's Thyroiditis (Autoimmune Hypothyroidism). Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014.

Measure heart rate: Check and document as baseline bedside data for this workup. Tachycardia, bradycardia, or irregular rhythm changes acuity, ECG need, and treatment urgency. Rationale: Screens severity and immediate safety findings that can change triage, monitoring, fluids, or urgent escalation for Hashimoto's Thyroiditis (Autoimmune Hypothyroidism). Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014.
clinical_questions: Have you had cold intolerance, constipation, fatigue, dry skin, slowed thinking, hoarse voice, weight gain, or heavy menses? Management: A positive answer raises concern for thyroid hormone deficiency or myxedema physiology and changes TSH/free T4 interpretation, replacement planning, and emergency-risk screening for Hashimoto's Thyroiditis (Autoimmune Hypothyroidism). Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014.

core_physical_exam_maneuvers: Palpate thyroid gland: Inspect and palpate the thyroid from in front or behind while the patient swallows, noting size, tenderness, asymmetry, nodules, fixation, or bruit if auscultated. Findings: Normal size / Goiter / Nodule/asymmetry / Tender / Fixed/hard / Unable to assess. Target: thyroid structural disease, compressive features, or thyroid inflammatory/autoimmune pattern Management: Goiter, tenderness, asymmetry, or a dominant nodule changes etiology and imaging decisions. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Palpate cervical lymph nodes: Palpate cervical and supraclavicular nodal chains systematically, noting size, tenderness, mobility, firmness, and laterality. Findings: No concerning nodes / Tender/mobile nodes / Firm/fixed nodes / Supraclavicular node / Unable to assess. Target: regional nodal disease or thyroid malignancy risk Management: Suspicious lymph nodes change ultrasound mapping, biopsy planning, and urgency. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Auscultate heart rhythm: Auscultate the precordium with diaphragm and bell as appropriate, noting rate regularity, extra sounds, murmurs, rubs, or gallop. Findings: Regular / Irregular / Tachycardic / Bradycardic / Unable to assess. Target: thyroid-related tachycardia, atrial fibrillation, bradycardia, or heart-failure complication Management: Irregular rhythm changes ECG need, beta-blocker consideration, and escalation. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment stethoscope

Observe outstretched-hands tremor: Ask the patient to extend both arms and spread fingers; observe for fine tremor and functional impact. Findings: No tremor / Fine tremor / Coarse tremor / Functionally limiting tremor / Unable to assess. Target: thyrotoxicosis adrenergic signs, medication effect, or endocrine-related neuromuscular irritability Management: Fine tremor supports adrenergic thyrotoxicosis and changes symptom-control decisions. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Listen to voice quality: Listen to voice quality during normal speech and ask about new hoarseness, weak voice, or compressive symptoms. Findings: Normal voice / Hoarse / Weak/breathy / Stridor/airway concern / Unable to assess. Target: thyroid structural disease, compressive features, or thyroid inflammatory/autoimmune pattern Management: Hoarseness changes concern for recurrent laryngeal nerve involvement and surgical urgency. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect neck for airway narrowing: Inspect neck contour and observe breathing/stridor; ask about positional dyspnea while avoiding provocative maneuvers in unstable patients. Findings: No visible narrowing / Large goiter/mass effect / Stridor/positional dyspnea / Unable to assess. Target: encephalopathy, crisis severity, or airway-protection risk Management: Airway concern changes urgency and imaging or procedural planning. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none
conditional_exam_add_ons: Inspect eyelid retraction: Inspect eyes and eyelids from the front and side for lid lag/retraction, proptosis, conjunctival injection, or exposure signs. Findings: Absent / Present / Asymmetric / Exposure concern / Unable to assess. Target: thyrotoxicosis adrenergic signs or Graves orbitopathy severity Management: Lid retraction supports Graves or adrenergic thyroid physiology and changes etiology assessment. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Test extraocular movements: Ask the patient to follow a target through cardinal gaze positions and observe for limitation, pain, diplopia, or nystagmus. Findings: Full / Restricted / Diplopia / Painful / Unable to assess. Target: Graves orbitopathy, diplopia, ocular motility restriction, or vision-threatening thyroid eye disease Management: Restriction changes orbitopathy severity and eye-care urgency. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect mucous membranes for dehydration: Inspect oral mucosa and tongue moisture with attention to dry mucosa, cracked lips, and overall hydration context. Findings: Moist / Dry / Very dry / Unable to assess. Target: volume status, dehydration severity, or peripheral perfusion Management: Dehydration changes fluid strategy, lab urgency, and monitoring needs. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

clinical_decision_tree: Start with TSH and free T4; high TSH with low free T4 supports overt primary hypothyroidism, while low free T4 with low/normal TSH suggests central disease. Rationale: Routes Hashimoto's Thyroiditis (Autoimmune Hypothyroidism) testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014.

Check TPO antibody when autoimmune thyroiditis changes prognosis, pregnancy planning, or etiology; ultrasound is reserved for goiter, nodule, asymmetry, or compressive symptoms. Source: AACE_ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2012.

results_that_change_management: Positive TPO Ab supports autoimmune thyroiditis. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Hashimoto's Thyroiditis (Autoimmune Hypothyroidism). Source: ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2014.

Suspicious nodule/compressive findings move to ultrasound/FNA pathway. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Hashimoto's Thyroiditis (Autoimmune Hypothyroidism). Source: AACE_ATA_HYPOTHYROIDISM_2012.

Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care. treatment_options: Treat overt primary hypothyroidism with levothyroxine, adjusted for age, cardiac disease, pregnancy, weight, adherence, and interacting medications. Rationale: Links confirmed Hashimoto's Thyroiditis (Autoimmune Hypothyroidism) results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. For suspected myxedema coma, use emergency IV thyroid hormone, stress-dose glucocorticoid coverage, warming, ventilation, sodium/glucose correction, and ICU-level care per local protocol. TSH with free T4; add T3 for thyrotoxicosis, TPO Ab for autoimmune hypothyroidism, TRAb/TSI for Graves, and thyroid ultrasound/FNA pathway for nodules.

red_flags: Thyroid storm: fever, severe tachycardia/AF, heart failure, agitation, GI symptoms, altered mental status.
Hemodynamic instability, altered mental status, severe electrolyte/glucose abnormality, arrhythmia, airway compromise, or vision threat requires urgent escalation.
Thyroid storm, myxedema coma, atrial fibrillation/heart failure, vision-threatening orbitopathy, rapidly enlarging neck mass, hoarseness, dysphagia, dyspnea, or suspicious nodes.
management_changes: Suppressed TSH plus high FT4/T3 confirms overt hyperthyroidism.
TRAb or uptake pattern changes etiology-specific treatment.
Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care.
Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment.
Suppressed TSH with elevated T4/T3 changes to thyrotoxicosis severity and etiology workup; low FT4 with low/normal TSH triggers central hypothyroidism evaluation.
Suspicious nodule, compressive symptom, abnormal node, calcitonin/RET concern, or high-risk ultrasound feature changes imaging/FNA/surgical referral pathway.
decision_steps: Confirm thyrotoxicosis with suppressed TSH plus elevated free T4 and/or T3, then determine etiology with TRAb/TSI, uptake scan, Doppler ultrasound, medication/iodine history, or thyroiditis labs.
Differentiate high-uptake hormone overproduction from low-uptake thyroiditis, exogenous hormone, iodine/amiodarone effect, or destructive thyroiditis because treatment differs.
Assess severity with heart rate/rhythm, fever, heart failure, mental status, liver disease, pregnancy, orbitopathy, and thyroid storm features.
For nodular or uncertain disease, evaluate autonomous nodules and malignancy-risk features before definitive therapy.
Confirm that Hyperthyroidism (Overactive Thyroid) is the selected clinical intent, then screen for red flags before routine outpatient testing.
Order the first-line tests in sequence: TSH, free T4, total/free T3; TRAb/TSI; RAIU or Doppler ultrasound when etiology unclear.

Interpret numeric results against the workup cutoffs and local assay ranges: TSH typical adult range about 0.4-4.0 mIU/L; use local lab, pregnancy, and illness context. Free T4 typical adult range about 0.8-1.8 ng/dL; use local assay.

If the initial result is equivocal, discordant with the phenotype, or affected by medication, acute illness, pregnancy, age, sex, or assay interference, repeat or confirm using the next guideline test before committing to long-term therapy.
treatment_options: Use beta-blocker symptom control when not contraindicated for adrenergic symptoms or tachycardia.
Use antithyroid drugs for Graves/toxic nodular hyperthyroidism when appropriate, with pregnancy trimester, liver disease, agranulocytosis counseling, and baseline labs considered.
Choose radioactive iodine, surgery, or continued medical therapy based on etiology, goiter/nodule features, orbitopathy, pregnancy plans, contraindications, and patient preference.
Treat thyroiditis primarily with supportive care, NSAID or glucocorticoid when indicated, and avoid antithyroid drugs when hormone release rather than synthesis is the mechanism.
Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Hyperthyroidism (Overactive Thyroid); draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization.
For confirmed Hyperthyroidism (Overactive Thyroid), choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway.
Monitor treatment response with the condition-specific clinical endpoint plus the most relevant labs or imaging listed in this workup.
Escalate to endocrinology or the relevant specialty when results are severe, discordant, recurrent, treatment-limiting, or require localization, surgery, genetic testing, or high-risk medication.

clinical_questions: Any palpitations, tremor, heat intolerance, anxiety, insomnia, weight loss, diarrhea, or sweating suggesting thyroid hormone excess or adrenergic physiology? core_physical_exam_maneuvers: Palpate thyroid gland: Inspect and palpate the thyroid from in front or behind while the patient swallows, noting size, tenderness, asymmetry, nodules, fixation, or bruit if auscultated. Findings: Normal size / Goiter / Nodule/asymmetry / Tender / Fixed/hard / Unable to assess. Target: thyroid structural disease, compressive features, or thyroid inflammatory/autoimmune pattern Management: Goiter, tenderness, asymmetry, or a dominant nodule changes etiology and imaging decisions. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Palpate cervical lymph nodes: Palpate cervical and supraclavicular nodal chains systematically, noting size, tenderness, mobility, firmness, and laterality. Findings: No concerning nodes / Tender/mobile nodes / Firm/fixed nodes / Supraclavicular node / Unable to assess. Target: regional nodal disease or thyroid malignancy risk Management: Suspicious lymph nodes change ultrasound mapping, biopsy planning, and urgency. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Auscultate heart rhythm: Auscultate the precordium with diaphragm and bell as appropriate, noting rate regularity, extra sounds, murmurs, rubs, or gallop. Findings: Regular / Irregular / Tachycardic / Bradycardic / Unable to assess. Target: thyroid-related tachycardia, atrial fibrillation, bradycardia, or heart-failure complication Management: Irregular rhythm changes ECG need, beta-blocker consideration, and escalation. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment stethoscope

Observe outstretched-hands tremor: Ask the patient to extend both arms and spread fingers; observe for fine tremor and functional impact. Findings: No tremor / Fine tremor / Coarse tremor / Functionally limiting tremor / Unable to assess. Target: thyrotoxicosis adrenergic signs, medication effect, or endocrine-related neuromuscular irritability Management: Fine tremor supports adrenergic thyrotoxicosis and changes symptom-control decisions. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect eyes for proptosis: Inspect eyes and eyelids from the front and side for lid lag/retraction, proptosis, conjunctival injection, or exposure signs. Findings: Absent / Mild / Marked / Vision/exposure concern / Unable to assess. Target: thyrotoxicosis adrenergic signs or Graves orbitopathy severity Management: Proptosis changes orbitopathy severity assessment and ophthalmology urgency. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Test extraocular movements: Ask the patient to follow a target through cardinal gaze positions and observe for limitation, pain, diplopia, or nystagmus. Findings: Full / Restricted / Diplopia / Painful / Unable to assess. Target: Graves orbitopathy, diplopia, ocular motility restriction, or vision-threatening thyroid eye disease Management: Restricted movement changes orbitopathy severity and urgent eye-care needs. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none
conditional_exam_add_ons: Inspect eyelid retraction: Inspect eyes and eyelids from the front and side for lid lag/retraction, proptosis, conjunctival injection, or exposure signs. Findings: Absent / Present / Asymmetric / Exposure concern / Unable to assess. Target: thyrotoxicosis adrenergic signs or Graves orbitopathy severity Management: Lid retraction supports Graves or adrenergic thyroid physiology and changes etiology assessment. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Listen to voice quality: Listen to voice quality during normal speech and ask about new hoarseness, weak voice, or compressive symptoms. Findings: Normal voice / Hoarse / Weak/breathy / Stridor/airway concern / Unable to assess. Target: thyroid structural disease, compressive features, or thyroid inflammatory/autoimmune pattern Management: Hoarseness changes airway and malignancy concern. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect mucous membranes for dehydration: Inspect oral mucosa and tongue moisture with attention to dry mucosa, cracked lips, and overall hydration context. Findings: Moist / Dry / Very dry / Unable to assess. Target: volume status, dehydration severity, or peripheral perfusion Management: Dehydration changes fluid strategy, lab urgency, and monitoring needs. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

clinical_decision_tree: Confirm thyrotoxicosis with suppressed TSH plus elevated free T4 and/or T3, then determine etiology with TRAb/TSI, uptake scan, Doppler ultrasound, medication/iodine history, or thyroiditis labs. Rationale: Routes Hyperthyroidism (Overactive Thyroid) testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Differentiate high-uptake hormone overproduction from low-uptake thyroiditis, exogenous hormone, iodine/amiodarone effect, or destructive thyroiditis because treatment differs. Rationale: Routes Hyperthyroidism (Overactive Thyroid) testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Assess severity with heart rate/rhythm, fever, heart failure, mental status, liver disease, pregnancy, orbitopathy, and thyroid storm features. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

For nodular or uncertain disease, evaluate autonomous nodules and malignancy-risk features before definitive therapy. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

red_flags: Thyroid storm: fever, severe tachycardia/AF, heart failure, agitation, GI symptoms, altered mental status.: Escalate urgently, reassess severity, and evaluate for dangerous mimics or complications. Rationale: Identifies dangerous Hyperthyroidism (Overactive Thyroid) presentations that should override routine outpatient workup and prompt urgent escalation. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Hemodynamic instability, altered mental status, severe electrolyte/glucose abnormality, arrhythmia, airway compromise, or vision threat requires urgent escalation.: Escalate urgently, reassess severity, and evaluate for dangerous mimics or complications. Thyroid storm, myxedema coma, atrial fibrillation/heart failure, vision-threatening orbitopathy, rapidly enlarging neck mass, hoarseness, dysphagia, dyspnea, or suspicious nodes.: Escalate urgently, reassess severity, and evaluate for dangerous mimics or complications. Rationale: Identifies dangerous Hyperthyroidism (Overactive Thyroid) presentations that should override routine outpatient workup and prompt urgent escalation. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.
results_that_change_management: Suppressed TSH plus high FT4/T3 confirms overt hyperthyroidism. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Hyperthyroidism (Overactive Thyroid). Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

TRAb or uptake pattern changes etiology-specific treatment. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Hyperthyroidism (Overactive Thyroid). Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Hyperthyroidism (Overactive Thyroid). Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Hyperthyroidism (Overactive Thyroid). Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Suppressed TSH with elevated T4/T3 changes to thyrotoxicosis severity and etiology workup; low FT4 with low/normal TSH triggers central hypothyroidism evaluation. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Hyperthyroidism (Overactive Thyroid). Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Suspicious nodule, compressive symptom, abnormal node, calcitonin/RET concern, or high-risk ultrasound feature changes imaging/FNA/surgical referral pathway. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Hyperthyroidism (Overactive Thyroid). Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.
treatment_options: Use beta-blocker symptom control when not contraindicated for adrenergic symptoms or tachycardia. Rationale: Links confirmed Hyperthyroidism (Overactive Thyroid) results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Use antithyroid drugs for Graves/toxic nodular hyperthyroidism when appropriate, with pregnancy trimester, liver disease, agranulocytosis counseling, and baseline labs considered. Rationale: Links confirmed Hyperthyroidism (Overactive Thyroid) results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Choose radioactive iodine, surgery, or continued medical therapy based on etiology, goiter/nodule features, orbitopathy, pregnancy plans, contraindications, and patient preference. Rationale: Links confirmed Hyperthyroidism (Overactive Thyroid) results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Treat thyroiditis primarily with supportive care, NSAID or glucocorticoid when indicated, and avoid antithyroid drugs when hormone release rather than synthesis is the mechanism. Rationale: Links confirmed Hyperthyroidism (Overactive Thyroid) results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Hyperthyroidism (Overactive Thyroid); draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization. Rationale: Links confirmed Hyperthyroidism (Overactive Thyroid) results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

For confirmed Hyperthyroidism (Overactive Thyroid), choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway. Rationale: Links confirmed Hyperthyroidism (Overactive Thyroid) results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Monitor treatment response with the condition-specific clinical endpoint plus the most relevant labs or imaging listed in this workup. Rationale: Links confirmed Hyperthyroidism (Overactive Thyroid) results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Escalate to endocrinology or the relevant specialty when results are severe, discordant, recurrent, treatment-limiting, or require localization, surgery, genetic testing, or high-risk medication. Rationale: Links confirmed Hyperthyroidism (Overactive Thyroid) results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

basic_bedside_data_safety_checks: Measure blood pressure: Check and document as baseline bedside data for this workup. Hypotension or severe hypertension changes acuity, medication safety, fluid strategy, and escalation. Rationale: Targets hemodynamic safety, hypertension or hypotension severity, and medication or fluid-safety context; positive, negative, or unable-to-assess findings guide escalation, diagnostic testing, treatment safety, or specialty planning for Graves' Disease. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Measure heart rate: Check and document as baseline bedside data for this workup. Tachycardia, bradycardia, or irregular rhythm changes acuity, ECG need, and treatment urgency. Rationale: Screens severity and immediate safety findings that can change triage, monitoring, fluids, or urgent escalation for Graves' Disease. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.
clinical_questions: Have you had heat intolerance, palpitations, tremor, anxiety, unintentional weight loss, diarrhea, or increased sweating? Options: No endocrine symptom / Heat intolerance/sweating / Cold intolerance/dry skin / Palpitations/tremor / Bowel change / Weight change / Other ___. Purpose: Defines symptom severity, tempo, complications, and whether Graves' Disease needs urgent evaluation today. Management: A positive answer raises concern for thyroid hormone excess or thyroid storm and changes beta-blocker need, thyroid testing urgency, trigger search, and emergency endocrine escalation for Graves' Disease. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Any eye pain, redness, light sensitivity, bulging eyes, double vision, reduced vision, or trouble closing the eyelids? Options: No eye/vision symptom / Eye pain/redness / Light sensitivity / Bulging/proptosis / Double vision / Reduced or peripheral vision change / Other ___. Purpose: Defines symptom severity, tempo, complications, and whether Graves' Disease needs urgent evaluation today. Management: The answer changes Graves orbitopathy severity assessment, urgent eye-protection steps, smoking counseling, and ophthalmology referral timing for Graves' Disease. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Do you currently smoke or recently smoke, especially in the context of Graves orbitopathy or cardiovascular risk? Options: Currently smoke / Recent smoking / Especially in the context of Graves orbitopathy / Cardiovascular risk / Other ___. Purpose: Defines the patient-specific context needed to interpret endocrine tests, avoid common mimics, and choose the safest next step for Graves' Disease. Management: The answer changes Graves orbitopathy severity assessment, urgent eye-protection steps, smoking counseling, and ophthalmology referral timing for Graves' Disease. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Is pregnancy possible now, planned soon, or recently postpartum, and would pregnancy change medication or imaging safety? Options: No relevant exposure / Medication change / Supplement use / Missed doses / Steroid or hormone exposure / Biotin / Iodine/contrast exposure / Other ___. Purpose: Identifies exposures, physiologic states, or treatment changes that can explain abnormal results or make Graves' Disease therapy unsafe. Management: The answer changes Graves' Disease screening timing, glucose-monitoring intensity, maternal-fetal surveillance, medication choice, and postpartum diabetes follow-up. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

How has weight changed from baseline, and was the change intentional or associated with appetite, fluid status, or systemic symptoms? Options: Today or acute onset / Days / Weeks to months / Chronic or baseline / Worse today / Unknown / Other ___. Purpose: Defines symptom severity, tempo, complications, and whether Graves' Disease needs urgent evaluation today. Management: The answer changes cardiometabolic risk stratification, prevention intensity, complication screening, and follow-up interval for Graves' Disease. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Any fatigue, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, hoarse voice, slowed thinking, depression, heavy menses, or weight gain suggesting hypothyroidism? Options: Not applicable / Pregnancy possible / Pregnant/postpartum / Cycle irregularity / Fertility goal active / Timing known / Unknown / Other ___. Purpose: Defines symptom severity, tempo, complications, and whether Graves' Disease needs urgent evaluation today. Management: A positive answer raises concern for thyroid hormone deficiency or myxedema physiology and changes TSH/free T4 interpretation, replacement planning, and emergency-risk screening for Graves' Disease. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Any anxiety, depression, irritability, slowed thinking, memory change, confusion, sleep change, or functional decline? When was the last menstrual period, how regular are cycles, and has bleeding changed from baseline? Any neck swelling, thyroid pain or tenderness, pressure, hoarseness, trouble swallowing, positional shortness of breath, or rapidly enlarging neck mass? Options: No respiratory symptoms / Cough / Sputum / Shortness of breath / Pleuritic pain / Hypoxemia or oxygen need / Other ___. Purpose: Defines symptom severity, tempo, complications, and whether Graves' Disease needs urgent evaluation today. Any childhood head/neck radiation, therapeutic radiation, occupational exposure, or family thyroid cancer risk factor? Options: No matching feature / Childhood head/neck radiation / Therapeutic radiation / Occupational exposure / Family thyroid cancer risk factor / Other ___. core_physical_exam_maneuvers: Palpate thyroid gland: Inspect and palpate the thyroid from in front or behind while the patient swallows, noting size, tenderness, asymmetry, nodules, fixation, or bruit if auscultated. Findings: Normal size / Goiter / Nodule/asymmetry / Tender / Fixed/hard / Unable to assess. Target: thyroid structural disease, compressive features, or thyroid inflammatory/autoimmune pattern Management: Goiter, tenderness, asymmetry, or a dominant nodule changes etiology and imaging decisions. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Palpate cervical lymph nodes: Palpate cervical and supraclavicular nodal chains systematically, noting size, tenderness, mobility, firmness, and laterality. Findings: No concerning nodes / Tender/mobile nodes / Firm/fixed nodes / Supraclavicular node / Unable to assess. Target: regional nodal disease or thyroid malignancy risk Management: Suspicious lymph nodes change ultrasound mapping, biopsy planning, and urgency. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Auscultate heart rhythm: Auscultate the precordium with diaphragm and bell as appropriate, noting rate regularity, extra sounds, murmurs, rubs, or gallop. Findings: Regular / Irregular / Tachycardic / Bradycardic / Unable to assess. Target: thyroid-related tachycardia, atrial fibrillation, bradycardia, or heart-failure complication Management: Irregular rhythm changes ECG need, beta-blocker consideration, and escalation. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment stethoscope

Observe outstretched-hands tremor: Ask the patient to extend both arms and spread fingers; observe for fine tremor and functional impact. Findings: No tremor / Fine tremor / Coarse tremor / Functionally limiting tremor / Unable to assess. Target: thyrotoxicosis adrenergic signs, medication effect, or endocrine-related neuromuscular irritability Management: Fine tremor supports adrenergic thyrotoxicosis and changes symptom-control decisions. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect eyes for proptosis: Inspect eyes and eyelids from the front and side for lid lag/retraction, proptosis, conjunctival injection, or exposure signs. Findings: Absent / Mild / Marked / Vision/exposure concern / Unable to assess. Target: thyrotoxicosis adrenergic signs or Graves orbitopathy severity Management: Proptosis changes orbitopathy severity assessment and ophthalmology urgency. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Test extraocular movements: Ask the patient to follow a target through cardinal gaze positions and observe for limitation, pain, diplopia, or nystagmus. Findings: Full / Restricted / Diplopia / Painful / Unable to assess. Target: Graves orbitopathy, diplopia, ocular motility restriction, or vision-threatening thyroid eye disease Management: Restricted movement changes orbitopathy severity and urgent eye-care needs. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none
conditional_exam_add_ons: Inspect eyelid retraction: Inspect eyes and eyelids from the front and side for lid lag/retraction, proptosis, conjunctival injection, or exposure signs. Findings: Absent / Present / Asymmetric / Exposure concern / Unable to assess. Target: thyrotoxicosis adrenergic signs or Graves orbitopathy severity Management: Lid retraction supports Graves or adrenergic thyroid physiology and changes etiology assessment. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Listen to voice quality: Listen to voice quality during normal speech and ask about new hoarseness, weak voice, or compressive symptoms. Findings: Normal voice / Hoarse / Weak/breathy / Stridor/airway concern / Unable to assess. Target: thyroid structural disease, compressive features, or thyroid inflammatory/autoimmune pattern Management: Hoarseness changes airway and malignancy concern. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect mucous membranes for dehydration: Inspect oral mucosa and tongue moisture with attention to dry mucosa, cracked lips, and overall hydration context. Findings: Moist / Dry / Very dry / Unable to assess. Target: volume status, dehydration severity, or peripheral perfusion Management: Dehydration changes fluid strategy, lab urgency, and monitoring needs. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

clinical_decision_tree: Confirm thyrotoxicosis with suppressed TSH plus elevated free T4 and/or T3, then determine etiology with TRAb/TSI, uptake scan, Doppler ultrasound, medication/iodine history, or thyroiditis labs. Rationale: Routes Graves' Disease testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Differentiate high-uptake hormone overproduction from low-uptake thyroiditis, exogenous hormone, iodine/amiodarone effect, or destructive thyroiditis because treatment differs. Rationale: Routes Graves' Disease testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Assess severity with heart rate/rhythm, fever, heart failure, mental status, liver disease, pregnancy, orbitopathy, and thyroid storm features. Rationale: Routes Graves' Disease testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

For nodular or uncertain disease, evaluate autonomous nodules and malignancy-risk features before definitive therapy. Rationale: Routes Graves' Disease testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Rationale: Routes Graves' Disease testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Rationale: Routes Graves' Disease testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

If the initial result is equivocal, discordant with the phenotype, or affected by medication, acute illness, pregnancy, age, sex, or assay interference, repeat or confirm using the next guideline test before committing to long-term therapy. Rationale: Routes Graves' Disease testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Rationale: Identifies dangerous Graves' Disease presentations that should override routine outpatient workup and prompt urgent escalation. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Rationale: Identifies dangerous Graves' Disease presentations that should override routine outpatient workup and prompt urgent escalation. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.
results_that_change_management: TRAb-positive biochemical hyperthyroidism supports Graves. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Graves' Disease. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Orbitopathy changes radioactive iodine risk and ophthalmology urgency. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Graves' Disease. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Graves' Disease. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Graves' Disease. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Suppressed TSH with elevated T4/T3 changes to thyrotoxicosis severity and etiology workup; low FT4 with low/normal TSH triggers central hypothyroidism evaluation. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Graves' Disease. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Suspicious nodule, compressive symptom, abnormal node, calcitonin/RET concern, or high-risk ultrasound feature changes imaging/FNA/surgical referral pathway. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Graves' Disease. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.
treatment_options: Use beta-blocker symptom control when not contraindicated for adrenergic symptoms or tachycardia. Rationale: Links confirmed Graves' Disease results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Use antithyroid drugs for Graves/toxic nodular hyperthyroidism when appropriate, with pregnancy trimester, liver disease, agranulocytosis counseling, and baseline labs considered. Rationale: Links confirmed Graves' Disease results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Choose radioactive iodine, surgery, or continued medical therapy based on etiology, goiter/nodule features, orbitopathy, pregnancy plans, contraindications, and patient preference. Rationale: Links confirmed Graves' Disease results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Treat thyroiditis primarily with supportive care, NSAID or glucocorticoid when indicated, and avoid antithyroid drugs when hormone release rather than synthesis is the mechanism. Rationale: Links confirmed Graves' Disease results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Graves' Disease; draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization. Rationale: Links confirmed Graves' Disease results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

For confirmed Graves' Disease, choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway. Rationale: Links confirmed Graves' Disease results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Monitor treatment response with the condition-specific clinical endpoint plus the most relevant labs or imaging listed in this workup. Rationale: Links confirmed Graves' Disease results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.

Escalate to endocrinology or the relevant specialty when results are severe, discordant, recurrent, treatment-limiting, or require localization, surgery, genetic testing, or high-risk medication. Rationale: Links confirmed Graves' Disease results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ATA_HYPERTHYROIDISM_2016.
