conditional_history_add_ons: How has height or growth velocity changed compared with prior measurements, family height pattern, and puberty timing? Options: Today or acute onset / Days / Weeks to months / Chronic or baseline / Worse today / Unknown / Other ___. Purpose: Defines the patient-specific context needed to interpret endocrine tests, avoid common mimics, and choose the safest next step for Amenorrhea (Absence of menstruation). Management: The answer changes pregnancy or reproductive safety, assay interpretation, medication choice, imaging timing, or referral urgency for Amenorrhea (Absence of menstruation). Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024.

Any weight loss, eating restriction, intense exercise, psychosocial stress, or change in energy balance that could suppress reproductive hormones? Options: No endocrine symptom / Heat intolerance/sweating / Cold intolerance/dry skin / Palpitations/tremor / Bowel change / Weight change / Other ___. Purpose: Defines symptom severity, tempo, complications, and whether Amenorrhea (Absence of menstruation) needs urgent evaluation today. Management: The answer changes cardiometabolic risk stratification, prevention intensity, complication screening, and follow-up interval for Amenorrhea (Absence of menstruation). Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024.

Any chemotherapy, radiation, pelvic/cranial surgery, gonadal surgery, or cancer treatment that could affect endocrine function? Options: No matching feature / Chemotherapy / Radiation / Pelvic/cranial surgery / Gonadal surgery / Cancer treatment that could affect endocrine function / Other ___. Purpose: Defines the patient-specific context needed to interpret endocrine tests, avoid common mimics, and choose the safest next step for Amenorrhea (Absence of menstruation). Management: The answer changes the leading Amenorrhea (Absence of menstruation) pathway by refining severity, mimics, confirmatory testing, treatment safety, and follow-up timing. Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024.

Any history of family premature ovarian insufficiency or PCOS that changes Amenorrhea (Absence of menstruation) risk, test interpretation, or treatment safety? Options: No matching feature / History of family premature ovarian insufficiency / PCOS / Test interpretation / Treatment safety / Other ___. Purpose: Defines the patient-specific context needed to interpret endocrine tests, avoid common mimics, and choose the safest next step for Amenorrhea (Absence of menstruation). Management: The answer changes reproductive-axis localization, pregnancy or fertility safety, androgen/prolactin/gonadal testing, and referral urgency for Amenorrhea (Absence of menstruation). Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024.
core_physical_exam_maneuvers: Inspect acne distribution: Inspect the relevant androgen-sensitive areas respectfully and document distribution, severity, and signs of virilization. Findings: Absent/minimal / Inflammatory acne / Severe/nodulocystic / Unable/deferred. Target: gonadal axis, prolactin, androgen excess/deficiency, or reproductive endocrine phenotype Management: Inflammatory acne supports hyperandrogenism and changes androgen testing threshold. Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect terminal hair distribution: Inspect the relevant androgen-sensitive areas respectfully and document distribution, severity, and signs of virilization. Findings: Expected distribution / Mild excess / Marked excess / Virilization concern / Unable/deferred. Target: gonadal axis, prolactin, androgen excess/deficiency, or reproductive endocrine phenotype Management: Terminal hair pattern changes hyperandrogenism grading and need for androgen evaluation. Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect neck for acanthosis: Inspect the named area directly, comparing sides when relevant and documenting presence, absence, severity, and inability to assess. Findings: Absent / Mild neck folds / Marked neck/axillary involvement / Unable to assess. Target: insulin resistance or cardiometabolic risk phenotype Management: Acanthosis changes metabolic-risk framing and diabetes screening urgency. Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect thyroid gland: Inspect and palpate the thyroid from in front or behind while the patient swallows, noting size, tenderness, asymmetry, nodules, fixation, or bruit if auscultated. Findings: Normal size / Goiter / Nodule/asymmetry / Tender / Fixed/hard / Unable to assess. Target: thyroid structural disease, compressive features, or thyroid inflammatory/autoimmune pattern Management: Goiter changes thyroid testing interpretation and differential diagnosis. Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect for galactorrhea: Inspect for spontaneous discharge and, only when clinically appropriate, gently express from the areola toward the nipple while preserving privacy and consent. Findings: Absent / Spontaneous discharge / Expressible discharge / Bloody/unilateral discharge / Unable/deferred. Target: gonadal axis, prolactin, androgen excess/deficiency, or reproductive endocrine phenotype Management: Galactorrhea changes prolactin testing and pituitary evaluation threshold. Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none
conditional_exam_add_ons: Inspect for clitoromegaly: Inspect the relevant androgen-sensitive areas respectfully and document distribution, severity, and signs of virilization. Findings: Absent / Present/possible / Rapid virilization concern / Unable/deferred. Target: gonadal axis, prolactin, androgen excess/deficiency, or reproductive endocrine phenotype Management: Virilization changes urgency of androgen tumor evaluation. Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect breast tissue: Inspect and palpate breast tissue to distinguish glandular tissue from adiposity and to identify discrete mass, tenderness, or discharge. Findings: No glandular tissue / Tender glandular tissue / Discrete mass / Nipple discharge/skin change / Unable/deferred. Target: gonadal axis, prolactin, androgen excess/deficiency, or reproductive endocrine phenotype Management: Suspicious breast findings change endocrine versus breast-mass pathway. Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect mucous membranes for dehydration: Inspect oral mucosa and tongue moisture with attention to dry mucosa, cracked lips, and overall hydration context. Findings: Moist / Dry / Very dry / Unable to assess. Target: volume status, dehydration severity, or peripheral perfusion Management: Dehydration changes fluid strategy, lab urgency, and monitoring needs. Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none
diagnostic_workup_and_reference_values: Pregnancy test first.: Interpret with local lab ranges. Reference anchors: Prolactin upper limit often about 20 ng/mL in men and 25 ng/mL in women, but lab, pregnancy, lactation, and macroprolactin matter. Male total testosterone should be early morning and repeated; lower limit often around 264-300 ng/dL depending assay. IGF-1, LH/FSH, estradiol, AMH, and pediatric growth labs require age/sex/puberty-specific ranges. TSH typical adult range about 0.4-4.0 mIU/L; use local lab, pregnancy, and illness context. Free T4 typical adult range about 0.8-1.8 ng/dL; use local assay. T3, TRAb/TSI, TPO Ab, and thyroglobulin Ab are assay-specific. Use local laboratory intervals and assay-specific cutoffs; endocrine immunoassays are affected by pregnancy, age, sex, acute illness, binding proteins, biotin, and medication effects. Rationale: Confirms the diagnosis, identifies mimics or complications, or establishes a baseline that changes treatment for Amenorrhea (Absence of menstruation). Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024.

questions: Duration trying, cycle regularity, ovulation symptoms.
Partner semen history, galactorrhea, thyroid symptoms, hyperandrogenism, prior loss.
What is the current trajectory, baseline status, prior diagnosis date, and what changed today?
Which medications, supplements, missed doses, recent procedures, acute illnesses, pregnancy/fertility context, or assay-interfering factors could change interpretation?
Ask about cycle pattern, pregnancy possibility, fertility goals, libido/sexual function, galactorrhea, hirsutism/acne/alopecia, hot flashes, medication exposure, and rapid virilization.
Clarify age/puberty timing, eating/exercise/stress, chronic disease, opioids/anabolic steroids, chemotherapy/radiation/surgery, family POI/PCOS, and psychosocial impact.
exam: BMI/BP, thyroid, hirsutism/acne, galactorrhea, testicular exam in male partner if in scope.
Vitals and acuity screen: BP, HR, respiratory status, temperature when acute, weight/BMI or waist when cardiometabolic risk matters.
Targeted complication screen guided by symptoms: volume/perfusion, mental status, skin/mucosa, neuromuscular findings, and organ-system complications.
BP/BMI/waist, hirsutism/acne/alopecia/acanthosis, thyroid, galactorrhea/breast findings when appropriate, secondary sex characteristics, and testicular/genital exam when in scope.
Visual fields/cranial nerve screen when headache, galactorrhea, hypogonadotropic pattern, or pituitary concern is present.
tests: Pregnancy test if amenorrhea.
TSH/prolactin.
FSH/estradiol/AMH/antral follicle count as indicated.
PCOS and androgen labs.
Semen analysis; male morning testosterone/LH/FSH if indicated.
Compare with prior results and repeat discordant nonurgent endocrine tests using correct timing, local assay, and interference precautions.

Infertility hormone safety tests: pregnancy test when relevant, ovulatory timing, TSH/prolactin, LH/FSH with estradiol or testosterone, AMH/ovarian reserve or semen analysis as appropriate, and urgent ectopic/red-flag testing for pain or bleeding.
Pregnancy test first when amenorrhea or pregnancy is possible; TSH, prolactin, gonadotropins/estradiol or testosterone/LH/FSH as syndrome-specific anchors.
Androgen testing with reliable assays for hyperandrogenism; metabolic screening for PCOS; semen analysis and partner factors when infertility is in scope.
reference_values: Prolactin upper limit often about 20 ng/mL in men and 25 ng/mL in women, but lab, pregnancy, lactation, and macroprolactin matter.
Male total testosterone should be early morning and repeated; lower limit often around 264-300 ng/dL depending assay.
IGF-1, LH/FSH, estradiol, AMH, and pediatric growth labs require age/sex/puberty-specific ranges.
TSH typical adult range about 0.4-4.0 mIU/L; use local lab, pregnancy, and illness context.
Free T4 typical adult range about 0.8-1.8 ng/dL; use local assay.
T3, TRAb/TSI, TPO Ab, and thyroglobulin Ab are assay-specific.
Use local laboratory intervals and assay-specific cutoffs; endocrine immunoassays are affected by pregnancy, age, sex, acute illness, binding proteins, biotin, and medication effects.
red_flags: Amenorrhea with visual symptoms, POI <40, severe hyperandrogenism, recurrent pregnancy loss needing specialist workup.
Hemodynamic instability, altered mental status, severe electrolyte/glucose abnormality, arrhythmia, airway compromise, or vision threat requires urgent escalation.
Positive pregnancy with pain/bleeding, rapid virilization, tumor-range androgens, severe abnormal bleeding, visual symptoms/headache, testicular mass, hard breast mass, or priapism.
management_changes: Anovulation, thyroid/prolactin abnormality, POI pattern, or male factor changes treatment/referral.
Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care.
Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment.
Pregnancy, fertility intent, rapid virilization, tumor-range hormones, or pituitary symptoms changes medication safety, imaging urgency, and referral pathway.
Biochemical hypogonadism, POI, hyperprolactinemia, PCOS, or thyroid disease changes counseling, bone/cardiometabolic prevention, and treatment selection.
decision_steps: Confirm duration, age, ovulatory pattern, pregnancy loss history, partner factors, and urgency criteria before endocrine-only testing.
Evaluate ovulation and endocrine causes with pregnancy test when amenorrhea, TSH, prolactin, ovarian reserve markers when indicated, PCOS/androgen labs, and midluteal progesterone when useful.
Include semen analysis and tubal/uterine evaluation rather than assuming an endocrine-only cause.
Escalate earlier for age, POI, severe oligo/azoospermia, amenorrhea, recurrent loss, known tubal disease, or cancer-treatment history.
Confirm that Infertility (Hormonal causes) is the selected clinical intent, then screen for red flags before routine outpatient testing.
Order the first-line tests in sequence: Pregnancy test if amenorrhea; TSH/prolactin; FSH/estradiol/AMH/antral follicle count as indicated.

Interpret numeric results against the workup cutoffs and local assay ranges: Prolactin upper limit often about 20 ng/mL in men and 25 ng/mL in women, but lab, pregnancy, lactation, and macroprolactin matter. Male total testosterone should be early morning and repeated; lower limit often around 264-300 ng/dL depending assay.

If the initial result is equivocal, discordant with the phenotype, or affected by medication, acute illness, pregnancy, age, sex, or assay interference, repeat or confirm using the next guideline test before committing to long-term therapy.
treatment_options: Treat thyroid disease, hyperprolactinemia, PCOS/anovulation, hypogonadism, or POI according to cause and pregnancy safety.
Use ovulation induction, typically letrozole for PCOS-related anovulation, in the appropriate reproductive pathway.
Use gonadotropins, dopamine agonists, surgery, ART, or male-factor treatment according to etiology and specialist guidance.
Avoid teratogenic medications and align treatment with pregnancy status, partner context, and patient goals.
Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Infertility (Hormonal causes); draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization.
For confirmed Infertility (Hormonal causes), choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway.
Monitor treatment response with the condition-specific clinical endpoint plus the most relevant labs or imaging listed in this workup.
Escalate to endocrinology or the relevant specialty when results are severe, discordant, recurrent, treatment-limiting, or require localization, surgery, genetic testing, or high-risk medication.

basic_bedside_data_safety_checks: Measure blood pressure: Check and document as baseline bedside data for this workup. Hypotension or severe hypertension changes acuity, medication safety, fluid strategy, and escalation. Rationale: Targets hemodynamic safety, hypertension or hypotension severity, and medication or fluid-safety context; positive, negative, or unable-to-assess findings guide escalation, diagnostic testing, treatment safety, or specialty planning for Infertility (Hormonal causes). Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024.

Measure heart rate: Check and document as baseline bedside data for this workup. Tachycardia, bradycardia, or irregular rhythm changes acuity, ECG need, and treatment urgency. Rationale: Screens severity and immediate safety findings that can change triage, monitoring, fluids, or urgent escalation for Infertility (Hormonal causes). Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024.

Measure body mass index: Check and document as baseline bedside data for this workup. BMI changes PCOS, hypogonadism, infertility, menopause, and cardiometabolic risk framing. Rationale: Quantifies anthropometric and cardiometabolic risk so abnormal values can change diagnostic criteria, treatment selection, dosing context, or prevention intensity for Infertility (Hormonal causes). Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024.

Options: Today or acute onset / Days / Weeks to months / Chronic or baseline / Worse today / Unknown / Other ___. Management: The answer changes reproductive-axis localization, pregnancy or fertility safety, androgen/prolactin/gonadal testing, and referral urgency for Infertility (Hormonal causes). Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024.

Are cycles predictably ovulatory, with mid-cycle symptoms, LH-kit evidence, luteal symptoms, or cycle tracking suggesting regular ovulation? core_physical_exam_maneuvers: Inspect acne distribution: Inspect the relevant androgen-sensitive areas respectfully and document distribution, severity, and signs of virilization. Findings: Absent/minimal / Inflammatory acne / Severe/nodulocystic / Unable/deferred. Target: gonadal axis, prolactin, androgen excess/deficiency, or reproductive endocrine phenotype Management: Inflammatory acne supports hyperandrogenism and changes androgen testing threshold. Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect terminal hair distribution: Inspect the relevant androgen-sensitive areas respectfully and document distribution, severity, and signs of virilization. Findings: Expected distribution / Mild excess / Marked excess / Virilization concern / Unable/deferred. Target: gonadal axis, prolactin, androgen excess/deficiency, or reproductive endocrine phenotype Management: Terminal hair pattern changes hyperandrogenism grading and need for androgen evaluation. Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect neck for acanthosis: Inspect the named area directly, comparing sides when relevant and documenting presence, absence, severity, and inability to assess. Findings: Absent / Mild neck folds / Marked neck/axillary involvement / Unable to assess. Target: insulin resistance or cardiometabolic risk phenotype Management: Acanthosis changes metabolic-risk framing and diabetes screening urgency. Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect thyroid gland: Inspect and palpate the thyroid from in front or behind while the patient swallows, noting size, tenderness, asymmetry, nodules, fixation, or bruit if auscultated. Findings: Normal size / Goiter / Nodule/asymmetry / Tender / Fixed/hard / Unable to assess. Target: thyroid structural disease, compressive features, or thyroid inflammatory/autoimmune pattern Management: Goiter changes thyroid testing interpretation and differential diagnosis. Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect for galactorrhea: Inspect for spontaneous discharge and, only when clinically appropriate, gently express from the areola toward the nipple while preserving privacy and consent. Findings: Absent / Spontaneous discharge / Expressible discharge / Bloody/unilateral discharge / Unable/deferred. Target: gonadal axis, prolactin, androgen excess/deficiency, or reproductive endocrine phenotype Management: Galactorrhea changes prolactin testing and pituitary evaluation threshold. Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none
conditional_exam_add_ons: Inspect for clitoromegaly: Inspect the relevant androgen-sensitive areas respectfully and document distribution, severity, and signs of virilization. Findings: Absent / Present/possible / Rapid virilization concern / Unable/deferred. Target: gonadal axis, prolactin, androgen excess/deficiency, or reproductive endocrine phenotype Management: Virilization changes urgency of androgen tumor evaluation. Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Measure testicular volume: Estimate testicular volume with orchidometer when available or careful exam, documenting asymmetry, tenderness, or mass separately. Findings: Expected volume / Small bilaterally / Asymmetric/mass concern / Unable/deferred. Target: gonadal axis, prolactin, androgen excess/deficiency, or reproductive endocrine phenotype Management: Small testes change primary versus central hypogonadism framing. Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment orchidometer optional

Inspect breast tissue: Inspect and palpate breast tissue to distinguish glandular tissue from adiposity and to identify discrete mass, tenderness, or discharge. Findings: No glandular tissue / Tender glandular tissue / Discrete mass / Nipple discharge/skin change / Unable/deferred. Target: gonadal axis, prolactin, androgen excess/deficiency, or reproductive endocrine phenotype Management: Suspicious breast findings change endocrine versus breast-mass pathway. Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect mucous membranes for dehydration: Inspect oral mucosa and tongue moisture with attention to dry mucosa, cracked lips, and overall hydration context. Findings: Moist / Dry / Very dry / Unable to assess. Target: volume status, dehydration severity, or peripheral perfusion Management: Dehydration changes fluid strategy, lab urgency, and monitoring needs. Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none
diagnostic_workup_and_reference_values: Pregnancy test if amenorrhea.: Interpret with local lab ranges. Reference anchors: Prolactin upper limit often about 20 ng/mL in men and 25 ng/mL in women, but lab, pregnancy, lactation, and macroprolactin matter. Male total testosterone should be early morning and repeated; lower limit often around 264-300 ng/dL depending assay. Evaluate ovulation and endocrine causes with pregnancy test when amenorrhea, TSH, prolactin, ovarian reserve markers when indicated, PCOS/androgen labs, and midluteal progesterone when useful. Rationale: Routes Infertility (Hormonal causes) testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: PCOS_GUIDELINE_2023.

Include semen analysis and tubal/uterine evaluation rather than assuming an endocrine-only cause. Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024.

Rationale: Identifies dangerous Infertility (Hormonal causes) presentations that should override routine outpatient workup and prompt urgent escalation. Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024.

Rationale: Identifies dangerous Infertility (Hormonal causes) presentations that should override routine outpatient workup and prompt urgent escalation. Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024.

Positive pregnancy with pain/bleeding, rapid virilization, tumor-range androgens, severe abnormal bleeding, visual symptoms/headache, testicular mass, hard breast mass, or priapism.: Escalate urgently, reassess severity, and evaluate for dangerous mimics or complications. results_that_change_management: Anovulation, thyroid/prolactin abnormality, POI pattern, or male factor changes treatment/referral. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Infertility (Hormonal causes). Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024.

Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care. Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment. treatment_options: Treat thyroid disease, hyperprolactinemia, PCOS/anovulation, hypogonadism, or POI according to cause and pregnancy safety. Rationale: Links confirmed Infertility (Hormonal causes) results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024.

Use ovulation induction, typically letrozole for PCOS-related anovulation, in the appropriate reproductive pathway. Rationale: Links confirmed Infertility (Hormonal causes) results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: PCOS_GUIDELINE_2023.

Use gonadotropins, dopamine agonists, surgery, ART, or male-factor treatment according to etiology and specialist guidance. Rationale: Links confirmed Infertility (Hormonal causes) results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ES_TESTOSTERONE_2018.

Avoid teratogenic medications and align treatment with pregnancy status, partner context, and patient goals. Rationale: Links confirmed Infertility (Hormonal causes) results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ASRM_AMENORRHEA_2024.

Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Infertility (Hormonal causes); draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization. Rationale: Links confirmed Infertility (Hormonal causes) results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: PCOS_GUIDELINE_2023.

For confirmed Infertility (Hormonal causes), choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway. questions: Duration, pain/tenderness, unilateral mass/nipple discharge.
Medications, cannabis/alcohol, liver/kidney disease, libido/ED/testicular symptoms.
What is the current trajectory, baseline status, prior diagnosis date, and what changed today?
Which medications, supplements, missed doses, recent procedures, acute illnesses, pregnancy/fertility context, or assay-interfering factors could change interpretation?
Ask about cycle pattern, pregnancy possibility, fertility goals, libido/sexual function, galactorrhea, hirsutism/acne/alopecia, hot flashes, medication exposure, and rapid virilization.
Clarify age/puberty timing, eating/exercise/stress, chronic disease, opioids/anabolic steroids, chemotherapy/radiation/surgery, family POI/PCOS, and psychosocial impact.
exam: Glandular vs adiposity, breast mass/skin/nipple/nodes, testicular exam, thyroid/liver signs.
Vitals and acuity screen: BP, HR, respiratory status, temperature when acute, weight/BMI or waist when cardiometabolic risk matters.
Targeted complication screen guided by symptoms: volume/perfusion, mental status, skin/mucosa, neuromuscular findings, and organ-system complications.
BP/BMI/waist, hirsutism/acne/alopecia/acanthosis, thyroid, galactorrhea/breast findings when appropriate, secondary sex characteristics, and testicular/genital exam when in scope.
Visual fields/cranial nerve screen when headache, galactorrhea, hypogonadotropic pattern, or pituitary concern is present.
tests: Total testosterone, LH, FSH, estradiol.
hCG if suspicious; consider AFP/testicular ultrasound if tumor concern.
TSH, prolactin, liver/kidney function.
Compare with prior results and repeat discordant nonurgent endocrine tests using correct timing, local assay, and interference precautions.

Gynecomastia safety tests: review medication/substance causes and check testosterone, estradiol, LH/FSH, beta-hCG, prolactin/TSH, liver/kidney function, and testicular evaluation when mass, rapid growth, or malignancy concern exists.
Pregnancy test first when amenorrhea or pregnancy is possible; TSH, prolactin, gonadotropins/estradiol or testosterone/LH/FSH as syndrome-specific anchors.
Androgen testing with reliable assays for hyperandrogenism; metabolic screening for PCOS; semen analysis and partner factors when infertility is in scope.

Use local laboratory intervals and assay-specific cutoffs; endocrine immunoassays are affected by pregnancy, age, sex, acute illness, binding proteins, biotin, and medication effects.
red_flags: Hard eccentric breast mass, nipple discharge, skin changes, nodes, testicular mass, rapid progression.
Hemodynamic instability, altered mental status, severe electrolyte/glucose abnormality, arrhythmia, airway compromise, or vision threat requires urgent escalation.
Positive pregnancy with pain/bleeding, rapid virilization, tumor-range androgens, severe abnormal bleeding, visual symptoms/headache, testicular mass, hard breast mass, or priapism.
management_changes: Testicular mass/hCG elevation changes to urgent tumor pathway; hypogonadism pattern changes endocrine workup.
Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care.
Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment.
Pregnancy, fertility intent, rapid virilization, tumor-range hormones, or pituitary symptoms changes medication safety, imaging urgency, and referral pathway.

decision_steps: Distinguish glandular gynecomastia from adiposity and from suspicious breast mass, then assess medications, substances, liver/kidney/thyroid disease, hypogonadism, and testicular symptoms.
Order testosterone, LH/FSH, estradiol, hCG, prolactin, TSH, liver/kidney tests, and testicular ultrasound when tumor or testicular abnormality is suspected.
Urgently evaluate hard eccentric mass, nipple discharge, skin/nodal changes, testicular mass, rapid progression, or elevated hCG/estradiol.
If pubertal, medication-related, or physiologic and low risk, observe with follow-up.
Confirm that Gynecomastia (Enlarged male breast tissue) is the selected clinical intent, then screen for red flags before routine outpatient testing.
Order the first-line tests in sequence: Total testosterone, LH, FSH, estradiol; hCG if suspicious; consider AFP/testicular ultrasound if tumor concern; TSH, prolactin, liver/kidney function.

Interpret numeric results against the workup cutoffs and local assay ranges: Prolactin upper limit often about 20 ng/mL in men and 25 ng/mL in women, but lab, pregnancy, lactation, and macroprolactin matter. Male total testosterone should be early morning and repeated; lower limit often around 264-300 ng/dL depending assay.

If the initial result is equivocal, discordant with the phenotype, or affected by medication, acute illness, pregnancy, age, sex, or assay interference, repeat or confirm using the next guideline test before committing to long-term therapy.
treatment_options: Stop or substitute causative medication/substance when feasible and treat underlying hypogonadism, thyroid, liver, kidney, or tumor cause.
Use reassurance and observation for mild recent physiologic gynecomastia without red flags.
Consider selective estrogen receptor modulator therapy for painful recent gynecomastia in appropriate patients with specialist input.
Refer for breast imaging, oncology/urology/endocrine, or surgery when malignant, testicular, persistent, severe, or distressing disease is present.
Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Gynecomastia (Enlarged male breast tissue); draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization.

For confirmed Gynecomastia (Enlarged male breast tissue), choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway.
Monitor treatment response with the condition-specific clinical endpoint plus the most relevant labs or imaging listed in this workup.
Escalate to endocrinology or the relevant specialty when results are severe, discordant, recurrent, treatment-limiting, or require localization, surgery, genetic testing, or high-risk medication.

Source: ES_TESTOSTERONE_2018.

Inspect hands for acral enlargement: Inspect and compare hands, fingers, ring/shoe-size clues, acral contours, and facial soft-tissue changes with prior photos or baseline when available. conditional_exam_add_ons: Test extraocular movements: Ask the patient to follow a target through cardinal gaze positions and observe for limitation, pain, diplopia, or nystagmus. Reference anchors: Prolactin upper limit often about 20 ng/mL in men and 25 ng/mL in women, but lab, pregnancy, lactation, and macroprolactin matter. IGF-1, LH/FSH, estradiol, AMH, and pediatric growth labs require age/sex/puberty-specific ranges. Use local laboratory intervals and assay-specific cutoffs; endocrine immunoassays are affected by pregnancy, age, sex, acute illness, binding proteins, biotin, and medication effects. results_that_change_management: Elevated IGF-1 plus inadequate GH suppression changes to pituitary MRI/surgical pathway. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Acromegaly. Source: ES_ACROMEGALY_2014.

treatment_options: Use transsphenoidal surgery as first-line for most resectable pituitary adenomas causing GH excess. Rationale: Links confirmed Acromegaly results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ES_ACROMEGALY_2014.

Use somatostatin receptor ligands, pegvisomant, dopamine agonists, or radiotherapy for persistent, unresectable, recurrent, or nonsurgical disease. Treat comorbidities such as sleep apnea, diabetes, hypertension, cardiomyopathy, colon neoplasia risk, and joint disease. reference_values: 8 AM cortisol <3 ug/dL strongly suggests adrenal insufficiency; >15-18 ug/dL often makes it unlikely, context dependent.
Cosyntropin peak cortisol <18 ug/dL is a traditional abnormal cutoff; modern assays may use about 14-15 ug/dL.
Sodium about 135-145 mmol/L; potassium about 3.5-5.0 mmol/L.
TSH typical adult range about 0.4-4.0 mIU/L; use local lab, pregnancy, and illness context.
Free T4 typical adult range about 0.8-1.8 ng/dL; use local assay.
T3, TRAb/TSI, TPO Ab, and thyroglobulin Ab are assay-specific.
Prolactin upper limit often about 20 ng/mL in men and 25 ng/mL in women, but lab, pregnancy, lactation, and macroprolactin matter.
Male total testosterone should be early morning and repeated; lower limit often around 264-300 ng/dL depending assay.
IGF-1, LH/FSH, estradiol, AMH, and pediatric growth labs require age/sex/puberty-specific ranges.
Use local laboratory intervals and assay-specific cutoffs; endocrine immunoassays are affected by pregnancy, age, sex, acute illness, binding proteins, biotin, and medication effects.
red_flags: Adrenal crisis, pituitary apoplexy, visual loss, severe hyponatremia.
Hemodynamic instability, altered mental status, severe electrolyte/glucose abnormality, arrhythmia, airway compromise, or vision threat requires urgent escalation.
Pituitary apoplexy, acute severe headache, visual field deficit, cranial nerve palsy, adrenal crisis, severe hypernatremia, inability to drink, or macroadenoma symptoms in pregnancy.
management_changes: Central adrenal insufficiency must be addressed before thyroid hormone escalation.
Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care.
Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment.
Central adrenal insufficiency must be addressed before thyroid hormone escalation; pituitary mass effect changes ophthalmology/neurosurgery urgency.
Confirmed hormone excess/deficiency plus MRI finding changes endocrine, neurosurgical, fertility, and replacement-therapy pathway.
decision_steps: Assess pituitary mass effect and emergency adrenal status first; check 8 AM cortisol/ACTH or dynamic testing when safe.
Evaluate axes with free T4/TSH, LH/FSH plus sex steroid, prolactin, IGF-1 and GH testing when indicated, and sodium/osmolality if DI is possible.
Obtain pituitary MRI for structural cause, prior tumor follow-up, or multiple axis deficits.
Never start or increase thyroid hormone in suspected central adrenal insufficiency until glucocorticoid status is addressed.
Confirm that Hypopituitarism is the selected clinical intent, then screen for red flags before routine outpatient testing.
Order the first-line tests in sequence: 8 AM cortisol +/- ACTH stimulation; Free T4 with TSH; LH/FSH with testosterone or estradiol.

Interpret numeric results against the workup cutoffs and local assay ranges: 8 AM cortisol <3 ug/dL strongly suggests adrenal insufficiency; >15-18 ug/dL often makes it unlikely, context dependent. Cosyntropin peak cortisol <18 ug/dL is a traditional abnormal cutoff; modern assays may use about 14-15 ug/dL.

If the initial result is equivocal, discordant with the phenotype, or affected by medication, acute illness, pregnancy, age, sex, or assay interference, repeat or confirm using the next guideline test before committing to long-term therapy.
treatment_options: Replace glucocorticoid deficiency before thyroid hormone, with stress-dose education and emergency steroid plan.
Use levothyroxine for central hypothyroidism titrated to free T4, not TSH.
Replace sex steroids, fertility therapy, desmopressin, or growth hormone selectively based on axis deficit, age, contraindications, and goals.
Treat tumor, apoplexy, visual compromise, or postoperative/radiation complications with endocrine-neurosurgical coordination.
Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Hypopituitarism; draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization.
For confirmed Hypopituitarism, choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway.
Monitor treatment response with the condition-specific clinical endpoint plus the most relevant labs or imaging listed in this workup.
Escalate to endocrinology or the relevant specialty when results are severe, discordant, recurrent, treatment-limiting, or require localization, surgery, genetic testing, or high-risk medication.

core_physical_exam_maneuvers: Test confrontation visual fields: Test each eye separately by confrontation, comparing peripheral fields with the examiner's field while the patient fixes on a central target. Rationale: Routes Hypopituitarism testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ES_HYPOPITUITARISM_2016.

Evaluate axes with free T4/TSH, LH/FSH plus sex steroid, prolactin, IGF-1 and GH testing when indicated, and sodium/osmolality if DI is possible. Rationale: Routes Hypopituitarism testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ES_HYPOPITUITARISM_2016.

Obtain pituitary MRI for structural cause, prior tumor follow-up, or multiple axis deficits. Rationale: Routes Hypopituitarism testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ES_HYPOPITUITARISM_2016.

Never start or increase thyroid hormone in suspected central adrenal insufficiency until glucocorticoid status is addressed. Rationale: Routes Hypopituitarism testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ES_HYPOPITUITARISM_2016.

Confirm that Hypopituitarism is the selected clinical intent, then screen for red flags before routine outpatient testing. Rationale: Routes Hypopituitarism testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ES_HYPOPITUITARISM_2016.

Order the first-line tests in sequence: 8 AM cortisol +/- ACTH stimulation; Free T4 with TSH; LH/FSH with testosterone or estradiol. Rationale: Routes Hypopituitarism testing and interpretation through a source-backed sequence so the next step follows the result, phenotype, and safety context. Source: ES_HYPOPITUITARISM_2016.

red_flags: Adrenal crisis, pituitary apoplexy, visual loss, severe hyponatremia.: Escalate urgently, reassess severity, and evaluate for dangerous mimics or complications. Rationale: Identifies dangerous Hypopituitarism presentations that should override routine outpatient workup and prompt urgent escalation. Source: ES_HYPOPITUITARISM_2016.

Hemodynamic instability, altered mental status, severe electrolyte/glucose abnormality, arrhythmia, airway compromise, or vision threat requires urgent escalation.: Escalate urgently, reassess severity, and evaluate for dangerous mimics or complications. Rationale: Identifies dangerous Hypopituitarism presentations that should override routine outpatient workup and prompt urgent escalation. Source: ES_HYPOPITUITARISM_2016.

Pituitary apoplexy, acute severe headache, visual field deficit, cranial nerve palsy, adrenal crisis, severe hypernatremia, inability to drink, or macroadenoma symptoms in pregnancy.: Escalate urgently, reassess severity, and evaluate for dangerous mimics or complications. Rationale: Identifies dangerous Hypopituitarism presentations that should override routine outpatient workup and prompt urgent escalation. Source: ES_HYPOPITUITARISM_2016.
results_that_change_management: Central adrenal insufficiency must be addressed before thyroid hormone escalation. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Hypopituitarism. Source: ES_HYPOPITUITARISM_2016.

Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Hypopituitarism. Source: ES_HYPOPITUITARISM_2016.

Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Hypopituitarism. Source: ES_HYPOPITUITARISM_2016.

Central adrenal insufficiency must be addressed before thyroid hormone escalation; pituitary mass effect changes ophthalmology/neurosurgery urgency. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Hypopituitarism. Source: ES_HYPOPITUITARISM_2016.

Confirmed hormone excess/deficiency plus MRI finding changes endocrine, neurosurgical, fertility, and replacement-therapy pathway. Rationale: Links a result or bedside finding to a concrete management change for Hypopituitarism. Source: ES_HYPOPITUITARISM_2016.
treatment_options: Replace glucocorticoid deficiency before thyroid hormone, with stress-dose education and emergency steroid plan. Rationale: Links confirmed Hypopituitarism results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ES_HYPOPITUITARISM_2016.

Use levothyroxine for central hypothyroidism titrated to free T4, not TSH. Rationale: Links confirmed Hypopituitarism results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ES_HYPOPITUITARISM_2016.

Replace sex steroids, fertility therapy, desmopressin, or growth hormone selectively based on axis deficit, age, contraindications, and goals. Rationale: Links confirmed Hypopituitarism results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ES_HYPOPITUITARISM_2016.

Treat tumor, apoplexy, visual compromise, or postoperative/radiation complications with endocrine-neurosurgical coordination. Rationale: Links confirmed Hypopituitarism results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ES_HYPOPITUITARISM_2016.

Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Hypopituitarism; draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization. Rationale: Links confirmed Hypopituitarism results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ES_HYPOPITUITARISM_2016.

For confirmed Hypopituitarism, choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway. Rationale: Links confirmed Hypopituitarism results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ES_HYPOPITUITARISM_2016.

Monitor treatment response with the condition-specific clinical endpoint plus the most relevant labs or imaging listed in this workup. Rationale: Links confirmed Hypopituitarism results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ES_HYPOPITUITARISM_2016.

Escalate to endocrinology or the relevant specialty when results are severe, discordant, recurrent, treatment-limiting, or require localization, surgery, genetic testing, or high-risk medication. Rationale: Links confirmed Hypopituitarism results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ES_HYPOPITUITARISM_2016.

questions: 24-hour urine volume, nocturia, thirst, access to water.
Lithium, hypercalcemia, hypokalemia, kidney disease, pituitary surgery/trauma/tumor.
What is the current trajectory, baseline status, prior diagnosis date, and what changed today?
Which medications, supplements, missed doses, recent procedures, acute illnesses, pregnancy/fertility context, or assay-interfering factors could change interpretation?

Ask about headache, visual field loss/diplopia, pituitary surgery/radiation/apoplexy, galactorrhea, libido/fertility/cycle changes, polyuria/polydipsia, growth/acral changes, and adrenal/thyroid symptoms.

Clarify mass-effect symptoms, pregnancy, medication causes of prolactin elevation, traumatic brain injury, childhood cancer history, and access to water/desmopressin when diabetes insipidus is possible.
exam: Volume status and orthostatic vitals, mental status, mucous membranes, neurologic/pituitary mass signs.
Vitals and acuity screen: BP, HR, respiratory status, temperature when acute, weight/BMI or waist when cardiometabolic risk matters.
Targeted complication screen guided by symptoms: volume/perfusion, mental status, skin/mucosa, neuromuscular findings, and organ-system complications.
Visual fields, pupils/EOM/cranial nerves when mass effect is possible; BP/orthostatics, volume status, mental status, and secondary sex/thyroid/adrenal signs.
Acral/facial/tongue/jaw/spacing findings, growth velocity/puberty staging in younger patients, and comorbidity signs such as sleep apnea, hypertension, diabetes, or cardiomyopathy.
tests: Confirm hypotonic polyuria: adult urine volume often >3 L/day or >40-50 mL/kg/day with urine osmolality often <300 mOsm/kg.
Serum sodium/plasma osmolality.
Exclude osmotic diuresis: glucose, calcium, potassium, renal function.
Water deprivation/desmopressin or copeptin-based test.
Compare with prior results and repeat discordant nonurgent endocrine tests using correct timing, local assay, and interference precautions.

Diabetes insipidus safety tests: check serum sodium/osmolality, urine osmolality/specific gravity, urine volume, glucose/calcium/potassium/renal function, and desmopressin, copeptin, or supervised water-deprivation pathway only when stable.
Pituitary-axis panel guided by presentation: AM cortisol/ACTH, free T4/TSH, prolactin, IGF-1, LH/FSH with sex steroid, serum/urine osmolality and sodium when DI is possible.
Pituitary MRI after biochemical evidence or mass-effect symptoms; dynamic testing such as cosyntropin, OGTT/GH suppression, water deprivation/desmopressin or copeptin testing when indicated.
reference_values: Serum sodium normal about 135-145 mmol/L; SFE inpatient CDI guidance: 146-149 mild, 150-159 moderate, >160 severe hypernatremia.
Urine osmolality <300 mOsm/kg is hypotonic; >800 often argues against DI.
Use local laboratory intervals and assay-specific cutoffs; endocrine immunoassays are affected by pregnancy, age, sex, acute illness, binding proteins, biotin, and medication effects.
red_flags: Hypernatremia, inability to drink, impaired consciousness, postoperative pituitary patient without desmopressin access.
Hemodynamic instability, altered mental status, severe electrolyte/glucose abnormality, arrhythmia, airway compromise, or vision threat requires urgent escalation.
Pituitary apoplexy, acute severe headache, visual field deficit, cranial nerve palsy, adrenal crisis, severe hypernatremia, inability to drink, or macroadenoma symptoms in pregnancy.
management_changes: Hypernatremia or impaired water access changes to urgent monitored fluid/desmopressin pathway.
Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care.
Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment.
Central adrenal insufficiency must be addressed before thyroid hormone escalation; pituitary mass effect changes ophthalmology/neurosurgery urgency.
Confirmed hormone excess/deficiency plus MRI finding changes endocrine, neurosurgical, fertility, and replacement-therapy pathway.
decision_steps: First confirm hypotonic polyuria: high urine volume with low urine osmolality, then check serum sodium and plasma osmolality.
Exclude osmotic diuresis and reversible nephrogenic contributors: hyperglycemia, hypercalcemia, hypokalemia, kidney disease, lithium, and diuretics.
Differentiate central DI, nephrogenic DI, and primary polydipsia using supervised water deprivation/desmopressin or copeptin-based testing when stable.
In postoperative or impaired-consciousness patients, treat hypernatremia and water access risk as urgent before elective diagnostic testing.
Confirm that Diabetes Insipidus is the selected clinical intent, then screen for red flags before routine outpatient testing.

Order the first-line tests in sequence: Confirm hypotonic polyuria: adult urine volume often >3 L/day or >40-50 mL/kg/day with urine osmolality often <300 mOsm/kg; Serum sodium/plasma osmolality; Exclude osmotic diuresis: glucose, calcium, potassium, renal function.

Interpret numeric results against the workup cutoffs and local assay ranges: Serum sodium normal about 135-145 mmol/L; SFE inpatient CDI guidance: 146-149 mild, 150-159 moderate, >160 severe hypernatremia. Urine osmolality <300 mOsm/kg is hypotonic; >800 often argues against DI.

If the initial result is equivocal, discordant with the phenotype, or affected by medication, acute illness, pregnancy, age, sex, or assay interference, repeat or confirm using the next guideline test before committing to long-term therapy.
treatment_options: For central DI, use desmopressin with careful sodium monitoring and education to avoid hyponatremia.
For nephrogenic DI, remove offending drugs when possible, treat hypercalcemia/hypokalemia, use low-solute diet, and consider thiazide/NSAID/amiloride strategies under specialist guidance.
For primary polydipsia, avoid empiric desmopressin unless clearly indicated and address psychiatric, medication, or behavioral drivers.
Treat hypernatremia or inability to drink with monitored fluids, desmopressin if central DI is likely, urine output tracking, and frequent sodium checks.
Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Diabetes Insipidus; draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization.
For confirmed Diabetes Insipidus, choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway.
Monitor treatment response with the condition-specific clinical endpoint plus the most relevant labs or imaging listed in this workup.
Escalate to endocrinology or the relevant specialty when results are severe, discordant, recurrent, treatment-limiting, or require localization, surgery, genetic testing, or high-risk medication.

basic_bedside_data_safety_checks: Measure blood pressure: Check and document as baseline bedside data for this workup. Hypotension or severe hypertension changes acuity, medication safety, fluid strategy, and escalation. Rationale: Targets hemodynamic safety, hypertension or hypotension severity, and medication or fluid-safety context; positive, negative, or unable-to-assess findings guide escalation, diagnostic testing, treatment safety, or specialty planning for Diabetes Insipidus. Source: ENDOTEXT_DI_2026.

Measure heart rate: Check and document as baseline bedside data for this workup. Tachycardia, bradycardia, or irregular rhythm changes acuity, ECG need, and treatment urgency. Rationale: Screens severity and immediate safety findings that can change triage, monitoring, fluids, or urgent escalation for Diabetes Insipidus. Source: ENDOTEXT_DI_2026.

Measure orthostatic blood pressure: Check and document as baseline bedside data for this workup. Orthostasis changes hypernatremia risk and need for monitored fluids. Rationale: Screens severity and immediate safety findings that can change triage, monitoring, fluids, or urgent escalation for Diabetes Insipidus. Source: ENDOTEXT_DI_2026.

Document mental status: Check and document as baseline bedside data for this workup. Confusion or somnolence changes hypernatremia severity, disposition, and monitored correction strategy. treatment_options: For central DI, use desmopressin with careful sodium monitoring and education to avoid hyponatremia. Rationale: Links confirmed Diabetes Insipidus results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ENDOTEXT_DI_2026.

For nephrogenic DI, remove offending drugs when possible, treat hypercalcemia/hypokalemia, use low-solute diet, and consider thiazide/NSAID/amiloride strategies under specialist guidance. Rationale: Links confirmed Diabetes Insipidus results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: SFE_CDI_2018.

For primary polydipsia, avoid empiric desmopressin unless clearly indicated and address psychiatric, medication, or behavioral drivers. Rationale: Links confirmed Diabetes Insipidus results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: ENDOTEXT_DI_2026.

Treat hypernatremia or inability to drink with monitored fluids, desmopressin if central DI is likely, urine output tracking, and frequent sodium checks. Rationale: Links confirmed Diabetes Insipidus results, severity, contraindications, and patient context to guideline-backed treatment options and monitoring. Source: SFE_CDI_2018.

For confirmed Diabetes Insipidus, choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway. questions: Cushing phenotype plus headache/vision symptoms.
Cyclic symptoms, exogenous steroid exclusion, menstrual/hypogonadal symptoms.
What is the current trajectory, baseline status, prior diagnosis date, and what changed today?
Which medications, supplements, missed doses, recent procedures, acute illnesses, pregnancy/fertility context, or assay-interfering factors could change interpretation?

Ask about headache, visual field loss/diplopia, pituitary surgery/radiation/apoplexy, galactorrhea, libido/fertility/cycle changes, polyuria/polydipsia, growth/acral changes, and adrenal/thyroid symptoms.

Clarify mass-effect symptoms, pregnancy, medication causes of prolactin elevation, traumatic brain injury, childhood cancer history, and access to water/desmopressin when diabetes insipidus is possible.
exam: Cushing phenotype, BP/glucose complications, proximal weakness/skin, visual fields.
Vitals and acuity screen: BP, HR, respiratory status, temperature when acute, weight/BMI or waist when cardiometabolic risk matters.
Targeted complication screen guided by symptoms: volume/perfusion, mental status, skin/mucosa, neuromuscular findings, and organ-system complications.
Visual fields, pupils/EOM/cranial nerves when mass effect is possible; BP/orthostatics, volume status, mental status, and secondary sex/thyroid/adrenal signs.
Acral/facial/tongue/jaw/spacing findings, growth velocity/puberty staging in younger patients, and comorbidity signs such as sleep apnea, hypertension, diabetes, or cardiomyopathy.
tests: First confirm endogenous Cushing with UFC, late-night salivary cortisol, or 1-mg DST cortisol >1.8 ug/dL.
ACTH: normal/high supports ACTH-dependent disease.
Pituitary MRI.
Inferior petrosal sinus sampling when MRI equivocal/discordant.
Compare with prior results and repeat discordant nonurgent endocrine tests using correct timing, local assay, and interference precautions.

Cushing safety tests: use late-night salivary cortisol, 1-mg dexamethasone suppression, or 24-hour urinary free cortisol after excluding exogenous steroids, and assess glucose, BP, potassium, infection/VTE, and bone risk to determine severity and treatment urgency.
Pituitary-axis panel guided by presentation: AM cortisol/ACTH, free T4/TSH, prolactin, IGF-1, LH/FSH with sex steroid, serum/urine osmolality and sodium when DI is possible.
Pituitary MRI after biochemical evidence or mass-effect symptoms; dynamic testing such as cosyntropin, OGTT/GH suppression, water deprivation/desmopressin or copeptin testing when indicated.
reference_values: Post-1 mg DST cortisol >1.8 ug/dL is a sensitive abnormal screen; late-night salivary and UFC cutoffs are assay-specific.
Use local laboratory intervals and assay-specific cutoffs; endocrine immunoassays are affected by pregnancy, age, sex, acute illness, binding proteins, biotin, and medication effects.
red_flags: Severe hypercortisolism with infection, hypokalemia, psychosis, VTE, uncontrolled hypertension/diabetes.
Hemodynamic instability, altered mental status, severe electrolyte/glucose abnormality, arrhythmia, airway compromise, or vision threat requires urgent escalation.
Pituitary apoplexy, acute severe headache, visual field deficit, cranial nerve palsy, adrenal crisis, severe hypernatremia, inability to drink, or macroadenoma symptoms in pregnancy.
management_changes: ACTH-dependent confirmed Cushing plus pituitary localization changes to pituitary surgery pathway; discordance triggers IPSS.
Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care.
Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment.
Central adrenal insufficiency must be addressed before thyroid hormone escalation; pituitary mass effect changes ophthalmology/neurosurgery urgency.
Confirmed hormone excess/deficiency plus MRI finding changes endocrine, neurosurgical, fertility, and replacement-therapy pathway.
decision_steps: Exclude exogenous glucocorticoid exposure first, including injections, creams, inhaled, topical, supplements, and medication interactions.

Screen with one of the recommended first-line tests: 1-mg overnight dexamethasone suppression, late-night salivary cortisol, or 24-hour urinary free cortisol, repeating or confirming abnormal results.
After endogenous hypercortisolism is confirmed, measure ACTH to separate ACTH-independent from ACTH-dependent disease.
Use adrenal imaging for ACTH-independent disease; for ACTH-dependent disease use pituitary MRI and inferior petrosal sinus sampling when imaging and biochemistry are discordant.
Confirm that Cushing's Disease (Pituitary-dependent Cushing's) is the selected clinical intent, then screen for red flags before routine outpatient testing.

Order the first-line tests in sequence: First confirm endogenous Cushing with UFC, late-night salivary cortisol, or 1-mg DST cortisol >1.8 ug/dL; ACTH: normal/high supports ACTH-dependent disease; Pituitary MRI.
Interpret numeric results against the workup cutoffs and local assay ranges: Post-1 mg DST cortisol >1.8 ug/dL is a sensitive abnormal screen; late-night salivary and UFC cutoffs are assay-specific.

If the initial result is equivocal, discordant with the phenotype, or affected by medication, acute illness, pregnancy, age, sex, or assay interference, repeat or confirm using the next guideline test before committing to long-term therapy.
treatment_options: Treat the cause when feasible: transsphenoidal pituitary surgery for Cushing disease, adrenalectomy for unilateral adrenal cortisol excess, or ectopic source-directed therapy.
Control severe hypercortisolism complications urgently, including infection, VTE, psychosis, hypokalemia, hypertension, diabetes, and heart failure.
Use steroidogenesis inhibitors, glucocorticoid receptor blockade, pituitary-directed drugs, radiation, or bilateral adrenalectomy when surgery is delayed, incomplete, impossible, or recurrent.
Provide postoperative adrenal insufficiency monitoring, glucocorticoid replacement/taper, recurrence surveillance, and cardiometabolic/bone risk treatment.
Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Cushing's Disease (Pituitary-dependent Cushing's); draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization.

For confirmed Cushing's Disease (Pituitary-dependent Cushing's), choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway.
Monitor treatment response with the condition-specific clinical endpoint plus the most relevant labs or imaging listed in this workup.
Escalate to endocrinology or the relevant specialty when results are severe, discordant, recurrent, treatment-limiting, or require localization, surgery, genetic testing, or high-risk medication.

core_physical_exam_maneuvers: Inspect skin for bruising: Inspect exposed skin and body habitus for easy bruising, wide purple striae, supraclavicular fullness, and related Cushingoid features. Findings: Absent / Present / Marked / Unable to assess. Target: hypercortisolism, Cushing phenotype, or glucocorticoid excess pattern Management: Easy bruising supports hypercortisolism phenotype and changes testing threshold. Source: ES_CUSHING_DX_2008; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Test chair-rise strength: Ask the patient to rise from a chair without using arms if safe, observing proximal strength and need for assistance. Findings: Normal strength / Mild weakness / Needs arms/assistance / Unable/unsafe to assess. Target: hypercortisolism-related proximal myopathy and functional severity Management: Proximal weakness changes severity assessment and treatment urgency. Source: ES_CUSHING_DX_2008; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect abdomen for purple striae: Inspect exposed skin and body habitus for easy bruising, wide purple striae, supraclavicular fullness, and related Cushingoid features. Findings: Absent / Present / Marked / Unable to assess. Target: hypercortisolism, Cushing phenotype, or glucocorticoid excess pattern Management: Wide purple striae increase clinical suspicion and justify hypercortisolism testing. Source: ES_CUSHING_DX_2008; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none
conditional_exam_add_ons: Test confrontation visual fields: Test each eye separately by confrontation, comparing peripheral fields with the examiner's field while the patient fixes on a central target. Findings: Full / Bitemporal deficit / Other field deficit / Unable to assess. Target: pituitary/sellar mass effect or cranial nerve involvement Management: Field loss changes emergency imaging and neurosurgical urgency. Source: ES_CUSHING_DX_2008; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Test extraocular movements: Ask the patient to follow a target through cardinal gaze positions and observe for limitation, pain, diplopia, or nystagmus. Findings: Full / Restricted / Diplopia / Painful / Unable to assess. Target: pituitary/sellar mass effect, cavernous sinus involvement, or cranial nerve III/IV/VI dysfunction Management: Ophthalmoplegia changes emergency evaluation and neurosurgical urgency. Source: ES_CUSHING_DX_2008; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none

Inspect mucous membranes for dehydration: Inspect oral mucosa and tongue moisture with attention to dry mucosa, cracked lips, and overall hydration context. Findings: Moist / Dry / Very dry / Unable to assess. Target: volume status, dehydration severity, or peripheral perfusion Management: Dehydration changes fluid strategy, lab urgency, and monitoring needs. Source: ES_CUSHING_DX_2008; LR+ n/a; LR- n/a; difficulty easy; 1 min; equipment none
